name: Semaglutide
description: A GLP-1 receptor agonist used for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management, notable for its long half-life enabling once-weekly dosing.
mechanismOfAction: Semaglutide is a modified GLP-1 analogue with 94% homology to native GLP-1. It binds to GLP-1 receptors on pancreatic beta cells, enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppressing glucagon release. It also delays gastric emptying and acts on hypothalamic appetite centers to reduce food intake.

overview: Semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist originally developed by Novo Nordisk for type 2 diabetes mellitus. It received FDA approval under the brand name Ozempic in 2017 for glycemic control and as Wegovy in 2021 for chronic weight management.

The peptide features a key amino acid substitution at position 8 (Aib) and a C-18 fatty diacid side chain attached via a linker at Lys26, which promotes albumin binding and dramatically extends its plasma half-life to approximately one week.
Clinical trials (STEP and SUSTAIN programs) demonstrated average body weight reductions of 15-17% in obese adults, along with cardiovascular risk reduction demonstrated in the SELECT trial.
researchContext: The SELECT cardiovascular outcomes trial (2023) showed a 20% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in overweight/obese adults without diabetes, expanding its therapeutic profile beyond metabolic disease.
Research is ongoing into semaglutide's effects on MASH/NAFLD, chronic kidney disease, and substance use disorders, with early data suggesting potential benefits in alcohol and opioid use reduction.
Oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) uses an absorption enhancer (SNAC) to achieve bioavailability through the gastric mucosa, representing a major advance in oral peptide delivery.
safetyNotes: Common adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and constipation, particularly during dose escalation. These are generally transient and diminish over weeks.
Contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 syndrome. Post-marketing reports include rare cases of pancreatitis and gallbladder events.

name: Tirzepatide
description: A dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for type 2 diabetes and obesity, achieving greater weight loss than GLP-1 mono-agonists in clinical trials.
mechanismOfAction: Tirzepatide is a 39-amino acid peptide that activates both GIP and GLP-1 receptors simultaneously. GIP receptor agonism enhances fat metabolism and may improve insulin sensitivity in adipose tissue, while GLP-1 receptor agonism reduces appetite and improves glycemic control. The dual mechanism produces synergistic metabolic effects exceeding those of GLP-1 agonists alone.

overview: Tirzepatide is the first-in-class dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist developed by Eli Lilly. It was approved by the FDA as Mounjaro for type 2 diabetes in 2022 and as Zepbound for chronic weight management in 2023.
The SURMOUNT clinical trial program demonstrated unprecedented weight loss results, with participants losing up to 22.5% of body weight at the highest dose (15 mg weekly) over 72 weeks.
Its C-20 fatty diacid moiety provides extended plasma half-life through albumin binding, similar to semaglutide's approach but with the added dimension of GIP receptor engagement.
researchContext: Head-to-head data from the SURPASS trials showed tirzepatide achieved superior HbA1c reduction compared to semaglutide 1 mg in type 2 diabetes. The SURMOUNT-5 trial directly compared tirzepatide to semaglutide 2.4 mg for weight management.

Ongoing research explores tirzepatide's effects on heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), obstructive sleep apnea, and MASH, with positive interim results in the SUMMIT and SYNERGY-NASH trials.
The dual-agonist mechanism has shifted the field toward multi-receptor targeting as a more effective strategy for metabolic disease.
safetyNotes: The adverse effect profile is similar to GLP-1 agonists: nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and decreased appetite are most common during dose escalation. Slow titration mitigates GI symptoms.
The same MTC/MEN2 contraindication applies as with GLP-1 agonists based on preclinical thyroid C-cell tumor signals in rodents.

name: Liraglutide
description: A once-daily GLP-1 receptor agonist approved for type 2 diabetes and weight management, the predecessor to semaglutide.
mechanismOfAction: Liraglutide shares 97% homology with native GLP-1 and features a C-16 fatty acid (palmitic acid) attached via a glutamic acid spacer at Lys26. It activates GLP-1 receptors to stimulate insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, delay gastric emptying, and reduce appetite through central nervous system pathways.

overview: Liraglutide was developed by Novo Nordisk and approved by the FDA as Victoza for type 2 diabetes in 2010 and as Saxenda at a higher dose (3.0 mg daily) for chronic weight management in 2014.
It was the first GLP-1 receptor agonist to gain an obesity indication and demonstrated average weight loss of approximately 8% of body weight in the SCALE trial program.
While largely supplanted by once-weekly options like semaglutide, liraglutide remains widely prescribed and serves as an important reference compound in the incretin class.
researchContext: The LEADER cardiovascular outcomes trial demonstrated a 13% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with type 2 diabetes and high cardiovascular risk.
Liraglutide has been studied in adolescent obesity (ages 12-17), receiving FDA approval for this population in 2020, making it one of the first GLP-1 agonists with a pediatric obesity indication.
Research has also explored liraglutide's neuroprotective properties, with the ELAD trial investigating its effects in early Alzheimer's disease.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, and injection site reactions. GI effects are most pronounced during initiation and dose escalation.
Carries the same class-wide boxed warning regarding thyroid C-cell tumors observed in rodents. Contraindicated in MEN2 or personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma.

name: Retatrutide
description: An investigational triple agonist targeting GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors, showing up to 24% weight loss in phase 2 trials.
mechanismOfAction: Retatrutide activates three incretin-related receptors simultaneously: GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon. GLP-1 and GIP agonism suppress appetite and improve insulin sensitivity. Glucagon receptor agonism increases energy expenditure through hepatic lipid oxidation and thermogenesis, contributing to greater fat mass reduction than dual agonists.

overview: Retatrutide is a triple-hormone receptor agonist developed by Eli Lilly, representing the next frontier beyond dual GIP/GLP-1 agonism. It targets GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon receptors in a single molecule.

In the phase 2 trial published in 2023, participants on the highest dose (12 mg) achieved a mean body weight reduction of 24.2% over 48 weeks, the largest reduction reported for any anti-obesity medication at that time.
The addition of glucagon receptor agonism is hypothesized to drive increased energy expenditure and hepatic lipid metabolism, addressing metabolic disease from both the intake and expenditure sides.
researchContext: Phase 2 data showed dose-dependent weight loss with acceptable tolerability. The phase 3 TRIUMPH program is evaluating efficacy and safety across multiple populations including those with type 2 diabetes and obesity.
Pre-clinical and early clinical data suggest significant reductions in liver fat content, making retatrutide a candidate for MASH treatment.
The glucagon component raises theoretical concerns about hyperglycemia, but clinical data show net improvements in glycemic control due to the counterbalancing GLP-1 and GIP effects.
safetyNotes: GI adverse events (nausea, diarrhea, vomiting) were common in phase 2 trials, consistent with the incretin class. Dose escalation protocols help manage tolerability.
Close monitoring of hepatic and metabolic parameters is ongoing in phase 3 trials given the novel glucagon receptor agonism component.

name: Survodutide
description: A dual glucagon/GLP-1 receptor agonist in development for obesity and MASH, emphasizing glucagon-driven energy expenditure.
mechanismOfAction: Survodutide activates both glucagon and GLP-1 receptors. GLP-1 agonism suppresses appetite and enhances insulin secretion, while glucagon agonism increases hepatic energy expenditure and promotes lipid oxidation. This dual mechanism targets both caloric intake reduction and increased metabolic rate.

overview: Survodutide is a dual glucagon/GLP-1 receptor agonist developed by Boehringer Ingelheim in collaboration with Zealand Pharma. It differs from tirzepatide by targeting glucagon rather than GIP alongside GLP-1.
In phase 2 trials, survodutide demonstrated up to 18.7% body weight reduction at 46 weeks and significant reductions in liver fat, positioning it as a potential treatment for both obesity and MASH.
The glucagon receptor component is designed to increase energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation, offering a complementary metabolic mechanism to appetite suppression.
researchContext: The SYNCHRONIZE phase 3 program is evaluating survodutide in MASH with fibrosis, obesity, and type 2 diabetes. Early results showed MASH resolution in a significant proportion of patients.
The glucagon/GLP-1 ratio in survodutide is tuned to maximize fat reduction while GLP-1 activity prevents glucagon-driven hyperglycemia.
Research interest is high in comparing glucagon/GLP-1 dual agonists (survodutide) against GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists (tirzepatide) for different metabolic profiles.
safetyNotes: GI adverse events are common, consistent with the incretin class. Nausea and diarrhea were the most frequently reported side effects in phase 2 trials.
Glucagon agonism requires monitoring for potential effects on blood glucose, although clinical data have not shown problematic hyperglycemia when balanced with GLP-1 activity.

name: Mazdutide
description: A dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist developed in China for obesity and type 2 diabetes, advancing through phase 3 trials.
mechanismOfAction: Mazdutide is a dual GLP-1 and glucagon receptor agonist based on oxyntomodulin. GLP-1 agonism reduces appetite and improves glycemic control, while glucagon agonism promotes energy expenditure through hepatic gluconeogenesis and thermogenesis. The balanced ratio aims to achieve weight loss through both reduced intake and increased expenditure.

overview: Mazdutide is a dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist co-developed by Innovent Biologics and Eli Lilly, based on the native hormone oxyntomodulin. It is primarily being developed in China with global expansion planned.
Phase 2 and phase 3 trials in Chinese populations have demonstrated weight loss of up to 14-15% over 48 weeks, with concurrent improvements in glycemic parameters.
The compound uses an acylation strategy similar to semaglutide to extend its half-life for once-weekly dosing.
researchContext: The GLORY phase 3 program in China has shown promising results in both obesity and type 2 diabetes populations. Mazdutide received conditional approval for obesity in China.
Comparisons with pure GLP-1 agonists are being explored to evaluate whether the glucagon component provides additional metabolic benefits beyond appetite suppression.
Research is also examining mazdutide's effects on liver fat and metabolic syndrome parameters.
safetyNotes: The safety profile is consistent with the incretin class, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting as the most common adverse events.
Dose titration helps mitigate GI side effects. Long-term safety data from phase 3 trials are being accumulated.

name: Cagrilintide
description: A long-acting amylin analogue in development for obesity, being studied both alone and in combination with semaglutide (CagriSema).
mechanismOfAction: Cagrilintide is a long-acting acylated analogue of human amylin. It activates amylin receptors (calcitonin receptor/RAMP complexes) in the area postrema and central nervous system to reduce appetite and food intake. It also slows gastric emptying and may reduce postprandial glucagon secretion, complementing GLP-1 agonist mechanisms through a distinct receptor pathway.

overview: Cagrilintide is a once-weekly amylin receptor agonist developed by Novo Nordisk. While studied as monotherapy, its primary clinical program combines it with semaglutide 2.4 mg in a fixed-dose combination called CagriSema.

The CagriSema combination targets two complementary appetite-regulating pathways: amylin signaling in the hindbrain and GLP-1 signaling in the hypothalamus, aiming for greater weight loss than either agent alone.
Phase 2 data showed CagriSema achieving approximately 17% weight loss at 32 weeks, outperforming the individual components.
researchContext: The REDEFINE phase 3 program is evaluating CagriSema across obesity populations, with results expected to position it against tirzepatide and other next-generation agents.
Amylin-based approaches represent a distinct mechanism from the incretin pathway, and the combination strategy reflects the trend toward multi-pathway metabolic interventions.
Cagrilintide monotherapy (REDEFINE 3) showed modest but significant weight loss compared to placebo, validating the amylin mechanism for obesity treatment.
safetyNotes: Nausea is the most common adverse event, consistent with both amylin analogues and GLP-1 agonists. The combination may produce additive GI effects during titration.
Pramlintide, the only approved amylin analogue, carries a risk of hypoglycemia when used with insulin; cagrilintide's longer half-life and different clinical context may alter this risk profile.

name: Orforglipron
description: An investigational oral non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist that could eliminate the need for injections in obesity and diabetes treatment.
mechanismOfAction: Orforglipron is a small-molecule, non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist that binds to and activates the GLP-1 receptor similarly to native GLP-1 peptide agonists. Unlike peptide-based GLP-1 RAs, its small-molecule structure allows oral absorption without the need for permeation enhancers, and it resists proteolytic degradation in the GI tract.

overview: Orforglipron is an oral, non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist being developed by Eli Lilly. Unlike oral semaglutide, which requires absorption enhancers and fasting restrictions, orforglipron's small-molecule design allows conventional oral dosing.
Phase 2 results demonstrated up to 14.7% body weight loss over 36 weeks in people with obesity, with a safety profile comparable to injectable GLP-1 agonists.
If approved, orforglipron could dramatically expand access to GLP-1-based therapies by removing injection barriers and potentially reducing manufacturing costs.
researchContext: The ACHIEVE phase 3 program is evaluating orforglipron in obesity and type 2 diabetes across multiple populations. Results are anticipated to determine whether oral non-peptide agonists can match injectable peptide efficacy.
The discovery of orally bioavailable small-molecule GLP-1 agonists represents a paradigm shift in incretin therapeutics, moving beyond the peptide engineering approach.
Competition in the oral GLP-1 space includes danuglipron (Pfizer) and other small molecules in early development.
safetyNotes: GI adverse events (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) were common in phase 2, consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonism. The daily oral dosing may affect the tolerability profile compared to weekly injectables.
No unique safety signals beyond the known GLP-1 class effects were identified in early trials.

name: Danuglipron
description: An oral small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist from Pfizer, under development for obesity and type 2 diabetes.
mechanismOfAction: Danuglipron is a small-molecule full agonist of the GLP-1 receptor. It binds the receptor's extracellular domain and transmembrane cavity, activating downstream cAMP signaling to suppress appetite, enhance insulin secretion, and delay gastric emptying through the same pathway as peptide-based GLP-1 agonists.

overview: Danuglipron is Pfizer's oral, non-peptide GLP-1 receptor agonist in clinical development. It is structurally distinct from orforglipron but targets the same receptor.
Early clinical data showed moderate weight loss efficacy with twice-daily dosing. Pfizer has pivoted to developing a modified-release once-daily formulation to improve adherence and tolerability.
The competitive landscape for oral GLP-1 agonists is intense, with danuglipron facing comparison to both orforglipron and oral semaglutide.
researchContext: Phase 2b results showed dose-dependent weight loss, but high discontinuation rates due to GI side effects prompted reformulation efforts.
The modified-release formulation aims to reduce peak plasma concentrations and GI adverse events while maintaining efficacy with once-daily dosing.
Pfizer's phase 3 program for the updated formulation is planned, though timelines have shifted due to the reformulation pivot.
safetyNotes: Nausea and vomiting were significant in early trials with the immediate-release formulation. The modified-release version aims to improve the therapeutic window.
Hepatic transaminase elevations were observed in some participants, requiring monitoring in ongoing trials.

name: Pemvidutide
description: A dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist in development for MASH and obesity, emphasizing liver-directed metabolic benefits.
mechanismOfAction: Pemvidutide is a dual GLP-1 and glucagon receptor agonist designed to balance appetite suppression via GLP-1 activity with enhanced hepatic lipid metabolism and energy expenditure via glucagon receptor agonism. The glucagon component promotes liver fat oxidation and may reduce hepatic steatosis.

overview: Pemvidutide is being developed by Altimmune as a once-weekly subcutaneous injection for obesity and MASH. It is a dual-agonist peptide targeting GLP-1 and glucagon receptors.
In the MOMENTUM phase 2 trial, pemvidutide demonstrated approximately 10-15% body weight loss and significant reductions in liver fat over 48 weeks.
The compound's mechanism emphasizes liver-directed benefits through glucagon-mediated fat oxidation, differentiating it from pure GLP-1 approaches.
researchContext: Pemvidutide is being positioned primarily for MASH/NAFLD, where the glucagon component may provide targeted hepatic benefits beyond what GLP-1 mono-agonists achieve.
The MASH-focused development strategy reflects the large unmet medical need in liver disease and the potential for glucagon agonism to directly address hepatic steatosis.
Head-to-head comparisons with GLP-1 mono-agonists and GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists will be important to define pemvidutide's therapeutic niche.
safetyNotes: GI adverse events are consistent with the incretin class. No unique safety signals have been identified in clinical trials to date.
Blood glucose monitoring is warranted given the glucagon component, though clinical data show net glycemic improvement.

name: MOTS-c
description: A mitochondrial-derived peptide involved in metabolic homeostasis and exercise mimetics, primarily studied as a research compound.
aliases: Mitochondrial Open Reading Frame of the 12S rRNA Type-c
mechanismOfAction: MOTS-c is a 16-amino acid peptide encoded by the mitochondrial 12S rRNA gene. It activates AMPK signaling, promoting glucose uptake and fatty acid oxidation in skeletal muscle. It also regulates the folate-methionine cycle and nuclear gene expression, functioning as a mitochondrial-derived retrograde signal that integrates metabolic stress responses.

overview: MOTS-c (Mitochondrial Open Reading Frame of the Twelve S rRNA Type-c) is a 16-amino acid peptide discovered by Changhan David Lee's laboratory at USC in 2015. It is encoded within the mitochondrial genome, making it one of the few known mitochondrial-derived peptides (MDPs).
In preclinical studies, MOTS-c has demonstrated exercise-mimetic properties: improving insulin sensitivity, enhancing glucose metabolism, and preventing diet-induced obesity in mice.
Circulating MOTS-c levels decline with age and metabolic disease, suggesting it may function as a biomarker and potential therapeutic target for age-related metabolic decline.
researchContext: Research has shown that MOTS-c translocates to the nucleus under metabolic stress to regulate adaptive gene expression via AMPK-dependent chromatin remodeling.
A small human study demonstrated that exercise increases circulating MOTS-c levels, supporting the hypothesis that it mediates some of the metabolic benefits of physical activity.
MOTS-c is being investigated as a potential intervention for age-related metabolic decline, though it remains in preclinical stages with no registered clinical trials for therapeutic use.
safetyNotes: MOTS-c is an endogenous peptide and preclinical studies have not identified significant toxicity. However, human safety data are extremely limited.
As a research compound without clinical trials, appropriate dosing, pharmacokinetics, and long-term safety in humans are not established.

name: Humanin
description: A mitochondrial-derived peptide with cytoprotective and neuroprotective properties, studied for aging and metabolic disease.
mechanismOfAction: Humanin is a 24-amino acid peptide encoded by the 16S rRNA region of the mitochondrial genome. It binds to IGFBP-3, BAX, and the FPRL1 receptor, inhibiting apoptotic pathways and promoting cell survival. It also interacts with the gp130/CNTFR/WSX-1 tripartite receptor complex to activate STAT3 signaling, providing cytoprotective effects across multiple tissues.

overview: Humanin was discovered in 2001 in a screen for genes that protect against Alzheimer's disease-related toxicity. It is a 24-amino acid peptide encoded in the mitochondrial genome's 16S rRNA gene.
Preclinical research has demonstrated that humanin protects against apoptosis induced by various stressors, including amyloid-beta, oxidative stress, and serum deprivation in cell culture models.
Circulating humanin levels decline with age and are inversely correlated with markers of age-related disease, suggesting a role as both a biomarker and potential therapeutic target for aging.
researchContext: Analogues such as HNG (S14G-humanin) have been engineered for greater potency and stability, showing enhanced neuroprotective effects in animal models of Alzheimer's disease.
Research connects humanin to the GH/IGF-1 axis: it may mediate some of the longevity benefits observed in growth hormone receptor deficiency states (Laron syndrome).
Studies in animal models have shown protective effects against atherosclerosis, diabetes, and cognitive decline, but human clinical trials have not been conducted.
safetyNotes: As an endogenous peptide, humanin is considered to have a favorable safety profile in preclinical models. No significant toxicity has been reported in animal studies.
Human pharmacokinetic and safety data are not available. The peptide's short half-life in circulation may limit therapeutic utility without analogue optimization.

name: AOD-9604
description: A modified fragment of human growth hormone (hGH 177-191) studied for fat metabolism and weight loss without the growth-promoting effects of full HGH.
mechanismOfAction: AOD-9604 is a modified 16-amino acid peptide corresponding to the C-terminal fragment (residues 177-191) of human growth hormone with an added N-terminal tyrosine. It stimulates lipolysis (fat breakdown) and inhibits lipogenesis (fat formation) through interaction with the beta-3 adrenergic receptor pathway, without affecting IGF-1 levels or promoting skeletal growth.

overview: AOD-9604 was developed by Metabolic Pharmaceuticals based on research by Frank Ng at Monash University. It represents the lipolytic domain of human growth hormone, isolated from HGH's growth-promoting effects.
The peptide received GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) status from the FDA in 2014 as a food supplement ingredient, though this is distinct from drug approval.

Despite promising preclinical data showing fat reduction without effects on growth or glucose metabolism, clinical trials in humans for obesity showed limited efficacy and the compound was not pursued for FDA drug approval.
researchContext: A phase 2b/3 clinical trial in obese patients failed to demonstrate statistically significant weight loss compared to placebo, which halted pharmaceutical development.
AOD-9604 remains popular in the peptide research community and compounding pharmacy space due to its theoretical mechanism and favorable safety profile.
Recent interest has focused on AOD-9604's potential for cartilage and joint repair, with Calzada conducting trials for osteoarthritis under a different indication.
safetyNotes: Clinical trials showed a favorable safety profile with no significant adverse events compared to placebo. AOD-9604 did not affect IGF-1, glucose, cortisol, or other hormonal parameters.
The lack of efficacy in clinical obesity trials, rather than safety concerns, was the primary reason for discontinued development.

name: Tesamorelin
description: A synthetic growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) analogue approved for HIV-associated lipodystrophy, reducing visceral fat.
mechanismOfAction: Tesamorelin is a synthetic analogue of GHRH with a trans-3-hexenoic acid modification that increases receptor binding stability. It binds to GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs, stimulating the physiological pulsatile release of growth hormone. The resulting GH elevation promotes lipolysis in visceral adipose tissue and improves body composition without directly elevating IGF-1 to supraphysiological levels.

overview: Tesamorelin is the only FDA-approved GHRH analogue, approved in 2010 under the brand name Egrifta for the reduction of excess abdominal fat in HIV-infected patients with lipodystrophy.
Unlike exogenous growth hormone, tesamorelin stimulates the pituitary to release GH in a physiological pulsatile pattern, preserving the body's normal feedback mechanisms.
Clinical trials demonstrated significant reductions in visceral adipose tissue (VAT) of approximately 15-18% over 26 weeks compared to placebo.
researchContext: Research has expanded beyond HIV lipodystrophy to explore tesamorelin's effects on NAFLD in HIV-positive patients, showing reductions in liver fat and fibrosis markers.
Studies have also investigated cognitive benefits, with data suggesting tesamorelin may improve executive function and verbal memory in older adults and HIV patients.
Tesamorelin is widely used off-label in the anti-aging and optimization space for its GH-stimulating properties.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include injection site reactions (erythema, pruritus), arthralgia, and peripheral edema. Fluid retention symptoms typically resolve or diminish over time.
Contraindicated in patients with active malignancy, pregnancy, or disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. IGF-1 levels should be monitored.

name: HGH Fragment 176-191
description: The C-terminal fragment of human growth hormone responsible for lipolytic activity, studied as a weight-loss peptide.
mechanismOfAction: HGH Fragment 176-191 corresponds to the tail end of the growth hormone molecule (amino acids 176-191) that is responsible for its lipolytic (fat-burning) effects. It mimics the way natural growth hormone regulates fat metabolism by stimulating lipolysis and inhibiting lipogenesis, without affecting blood sugar or growth patterns as full-length HGH does.

overview: HGH Fragment 176-191 is the unmodified 16-amino acid C-terminal segment of human growth hormone. Research from Monash University identified this region as containing the lipolytic activity of the full 191-amino acid HGH molecule.

Unlike full-length growth hormone, this fragment does not compete for the GH receptor in a way that stimulates IGF-1 production or promotes linear growth, theoretically isolating the fat-metabolism effects.
It is closely related to AOD-9604, which is the same sequence with an additional N-terminal tyrosine for stability.
researchContext: Preclinical studies in obese mice showed that the fragment reduced body fat without the diabetogenic effects of full-length growth hormone.
The fragment has not undergone rigorous human clinical trials as a standalone therapeutic, though AOD-9604 (the modified version) did progress through clinical development.
It remains a widely discussed research compound in the body composition optimization space.
safetyNotes: Limited human safety data exist. Preclinical studies suggest it does not affect glucose metabolism or induce insulin resistance, unlike full-length HGH.
Users should be aware that the unmodified fragment has very short plasma stability, which may limit its practical utility.

name: Ipamorelin
description: A selective growth hormone secretagogue that stimulates GH release from the pituitary with minimal effects on cortisol and prolactin.
mechanismOfAction: Ipamorelin is a pentapeptide growth hormone secretagogue that selectively binds the ghrelin/GHS receptor (GHSR) on pituitary somatotrophs, stimulating GH release in a dose-dependent manner. Unlike GHRP-6 and GHRP-2, it does not significantly elevate cortisol, ACTH, prolactin, or aldosterone at therapeutic doses, making it the most selective GHSR agonist in its class.

overview: Ipamorelin was developed by Novo Nordisk in the late 1990s as part of a growth hormone secretagogue program. It is a pentapeptide that selectively stimulates GH release through the ghrelin receptor.

Its hallmark feature is selectivity: unlike earlier GHRPs, ipamorelin produces GH release without significant increases in cortisol or prolactin, making it appealing for body composition and recovery applications.
It is commonly used in combination with CJC-1295 (with or without DAC) to create a sustained GH elevation pattern that mimics physiological GH pulsatility.
researchContext: Preclinical studies demonstrated that ipamorelin increased bone mineral density and lean body mass in aged rats, suggesting potential applications in sarcopenia and osteoporosis.
A clinical trial investigated ipamorelin for post-operative ileus recovery after abdominal surgery, showing trends toward faster GI recovery though it did not meet primary endpoints.
Research on the ipamorelin/CJC-1295 combination is largely observational from the clinical optimization space, as formal pharmaceutical development was not pursued.
safetyNotes: Ipamorelin is generally well-tolerated in clinical and research settings. Its selectivity profile minimizes cortisol and prolactin-related side effects seen with other GHRPs.
Potential side effects include transient flushing, headache, and water retention. Long-term safety data from controlled human trials are limited.

name: CJC-1295
description: A synthetic GHRH analogue that extends growth hormone release duration, available with or without Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) modification.
mechanismOfAction: CJC-1295 is a synthetic analogue of GHRH (growth hormone-releasing hormone) consisting of the first 29 amino acids of GHRH with four amino acid substitutions (Ala2→D-Ala, Asn8→Gln, Ala15→Ala(NH-hexanoic), Met27→Leu) that improve metabolic stability. The DAC version covalently binds to serum albumin via a reactive lysine-maleimide linker, dramatically extending half-life. It stimulates pulsatile GH release via GHRH receptors on pituitary somatotrophs.

overview: CJC-1295 was developed by ConjuChem Biotechnologies as a long-acting GHRH analogue. The 'no DAC' version (also called Modified GRF 1-29) has a short half-life similar to native GHRH, while the DAC version achieves multi-day duration through albumin conjugation.
The no-DAC version is typically combined with ipamorelin for pulsatile GH stimulation that mimics natural GH release patterns. The DAC version produces more sustained but less pulsatile GH elevation.
It is one of the most widely used growth hormone peptides in clinical optimization and anti-aging medicine.
researchContext: Clinical studies of CJC-1295 DAC showed sustained IGF-1 elevation for 6-14 days after a single injection, with dose-dependent GH increases.
A death during a clinical trial of CJC-1295 DAC was attributed to unrelated causes, but it contributed to halting pharmaceutical development.
The no-DAC version (Mod GRF 1-29) has become the preferred form in the optimization space due to more physiological pulsatile GH release patterns.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include injection site reactions, flushing, and headache. Water retention and paresthesias can occur with sustained GH elevation.
The DAC version's prolonged action means side effects may persist longer. IGF-1 monitoring is recommended with regular use.

references.title: Prolonged Stimulation of Growth Hormone (GH) and Insulin-Like Growth Factor I Secretion by CJC-1295
Effect of CJC-1295, a Long-Acting GHRH Analog, on GH and IGF-I Secretion in Healthy Adults

contentJson.hubNote: CJC-1295 is the foundational GHRH analogue on Peptide Daily, presented alongside ipamorelin as the canonical GH-stimulating stack.

name: Sermorelin
description: A synthetic GHRH analogue consisting of the first 29 amino acids of native GHRH, previously FDA-approved for pediatric growth hormone deficiency.
mechanismOfAction: Sermorelin is identical to the first 29 amino acids of endogenous 44-amino acid GHRH, which retains full biological activity at the GHRH receptor. It stimulates pituitary somatotrophs to synthesize and release growth hormone through cAMP-mediated signaling, maintaining physiological GH pulsatility and negative feedback regulation.

overview: Sermorelin (GHRH 1-29) was FDA-approved in 1997 under the brand name Geref for the diagnosis and treatment of growth hormone deficiency in children. The branded product was discontinued in 2008 for commercial reasons, not safety concerns.
It remains available through compounding pharmacies and is one of the most established GHRH analogues in clinical use for adult GH optimization.
Its short half-life requires daily or twice-daily dosing, typically administered before bedtime to augment the natural nocturnal GH pulse.
researchContext: Studies in GH-deficient adults showed sermorelin normalized IGF-1 levels and improved body composition, sleep quality, and recovery markers.
Sermorelin's physiological mechanism preserves the pituitary's negative feedback loop, meaning GH release is self-limiting unlike exogenous HGH administration.
Compared to modified analogues like CJC-1295, sermorelin has the advantage of a longer safety track record but requires more frequent dosing.
safetyNotes: Sermorelin has the longest clinical safety record among GHRH analogues. Common side effects include injection site reactions, facial flushing, and headache.
Serious adverse events are rare. It is contraindicated in patients with active malignancies or untreated hypothalamic-pituitary lesions.

name: GHRP-6
description: A growth hormone-releasing peptide that stimulates GH secretion through the ghrelin receptor, known for potent appetite-stimulating effects.
mechanismOfAction: GHRP-6 is a hexapeptide that acts as a potent agonist at the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHSR/ghrelin receptor) on pituitary somatotrophs and hypothalamic neurons. It stimulates GH release through both direct pituitary action and amplification of GHRH signaling. Unlike ipamorelin, GHRP-6 also significantly increases cortisol, prolactin, and ghrelin levels, producing notable appetite stimulation.

overview: GHRP-6 was one of the first synthetic growth hormone secretagogues developed, emerging from Cyril Bowers' pioneering work on met-enkephalin analogues that stimulate GH release in the 1980s.
It produces robust GH release but is less selective than newer secretagogues like ipamorelin, causing concurrent elevations in cortisol, prolactin, and appetite-stimulating ghrelin.
Its strong appetite-stimulating effect can be beneficial for patients needing caloric intake support but is undesirable for those seeking body composition improvement.
researchContext: GHRP-6 has been extensively studied as a research tool for understanding ghrelin receptor biology and GH axis regulation.
Preclinical studies showed cardioprotective effects through anti-fibrotic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms independent of GH release.
It is often compared to GHRP-2, which has similar potency but moderately less appetite stimulation, and to ipamorelin, which is far more selective.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include intense hunger, water retention, cortisol elevation, and potential prolactin elevation. The hunger effect can be significant.
Not recommended for individuals sensitive to cortisol fluctuations or those with prolactin-sensitive conditions.

Cardioprotective Effects of Growth Hormone-Releasing Peptide 6

contentJson.hubNote: GHRP-6 is positioned on Peptide Daily as the classical GH secretagogue, with comparisons to more selective alternatives.

name: GHRP-2
description: A potent growth hormone-releasing peptide with stronger GH stimulation than GHRP-6 and moderately less appetite stimulation.
mechanismOfAction: GHRP-2 is a synthetic hexapeptide that agonizes the GHSR (ghrelin receptor) to stimulate GH release from the anterior pituitary. It is considered the most potent of the classical GHRPs in terms of GH output per dose. It elevates cortisol and prolactin less than GHRP-6 but more than ipamorelin, placing it between the two in selectivity.

overview: GHRP-2 (also known as pralmorelin) was developed as an improvement over GHRP-6, producing greater GH release with somewhat reduced cortisol and prolactin stimulation.
It is approved in Japan under the name pralmorelin as a diagnostic agent for growth hormone deficiency, making it one of the few GHRPs with any regulatory approval.
GHRP-2 produces moderate appetite stimulation via ghrelin receptor activation, though less intensely than GHRP-6.
researchContext: Clinical studies have used GHRP-2 as a GH stimulation test agent, demonstrating reliable and robust GH release in both adults and children.
Research has explored synergy between GHRP-2 and GHRH analogues, showing that combined administration produces GH release greater than either agent alone.
GHRP-2 has been studied for potential cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory effects in GI models, similar to GHRP-6's cardioprotective properties.
safetyNotes: Side effects include hunger, water retention, and mild cortisol and prolactin elevation. These effects are generally less pronounced than with GHRP-6.
Long-term safety data from clinical use are available primarily from the Japanese diagnostic setting, where it is administered in single doses.

name: Hexarelin
description: A potent growth hormone-releasing hexapeptide with notable cardioprotective properties independent of GH release.
mechanismOfAction: Hexarelin is a synthetic hexapeptide that activates the GHSR to stimulate GH release. Beyond the GH axis, hexarelin binds to cardiac CD36 receptors (scavenger receptor class B), producing cardioprotective effects including improved cardiac output, coronary perfusion, and anti-fibrotic activity. It is the most potent GH secretagogue of the classical GHRP series but also shows the most desensitization with chronic use.

overview: Hexarelin (examorelin) is a synthetic hexapeptide growth hormone secretagogue developed in the 1990s. It produces the highest GH peak of any classical GHRP but is prone to receptor desensitization with continuous use.
Its most distinctive feature is direct cardioprotective activity mediated through CD36 receptors on cardiomyocytes, which is independent of and additive to its GH-releasing effects.
Due to tachyphylaxis concerns, hexarelin is typically used in cycling protocols rather than continuous daily administration.
researchContext: Clinical studies in patients with growth hormone deficiency and in cardiac patients demonstrated significant GH release and improvements in cardiac function parameters.
The cardioprotective mechanism via CD36 has been confirmed in preclinical models of cardiac ischemia-reperfusion injury and heart failure.
Hexarelin was evaluated in phase 2 trials for heart failure but was not advanced to phase 3 clinical development.
safetyNotes: Hexarelin elevates cortisol and prolactin more than ipamorelin but has a generally acceptable safety profile in clinical studies. Common side effects include flushing and hunger.
The desensitization of GH response with chronic use is a practical limitation; cycling protocols are recommended to maintain efficacy.

name: MK-677
description: An orally active, non-peptide growth hormone secretagogue that mimics ghrelin to stimulate sustained GH and IGF-1 elevation.
mechanismOfAction: MK-677 (ibutamoren) is a non-peptide spiropiperidine that functions as a potent, long-acting oral agonist of the GHSR (ghrelin receptor). It produces sustained elevation of GH, IGF-1, and IGFBP-3 over 24 hours without affecting cortisol levels. Unlike exogenous GH, it preserves the pulsatile pattern of GH release and maintains the hypothalamic-pituitary feedback axis.

overview: MK-677 (ibutamoren) was developed by Merck as an oral growth hormone secretagogue. Despite extensive clinical research, it has not received FDA approval for any indication, though it remains in investigational status.
Its oral bioavailability and 24-hour duration of action make it unique among GH secretagogues. A single daily dose produces sustained increases in GH, IGF-1, and IGFBP-3.
Clinical studies spanning up to 2 years have demonstrated consistent increases in IGF-1 to youthful levels in elderly subjects, along with improvements in body composition and bone density markers.
researchContext: A two-year study in elderly adults showed sustained IGF-1 elevation, increased fat-free mass, and improved bone mineral density without serious adverse effects.
MK-677 has been studied for hip fracture recovery, sarcopenia, sleep quality, and growth hormone deficiency, with generally positive but insufficient data for regulatory approval.
Its mechanism has been valuable for understanding ghrelin receptor biology and the long-term effects of sustained GH axis stimulation.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include increased appetite, transient water retention, muscle pain, and increased fasting blood glucose. Insulin resistance is a concern with long-term use.
Blood glucose and insulin levels should be monitored, especially in individuals with pre-existing insulin resistance or diabetes risk factors.

references.title: Two-Year Effects of Ibutamoren (MK-677) on Body Composition, GH and IGF-1 in Elderly Adults
MK-677, an Orally Active Growth Hormone Secretagogue, Reverses Diet-Induced Catabolism

contentJson.hubNote: MK-677 is the most-discussed oral GH secretagogue on Peptide Daily, bridging the peptide and small-molecule categories.

name: Alexamorelin
description: A synthetic heptapeptide growth hormone secretagogue with improved potency over earlier GHRPs, studied for GH deficiency.
mechanismOfAction: Alexamorelin is a synthetic heptapeptide that activates the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHSR) to stimulate GH release from pituitary somatotrophs. It was designed as an optimized GHRP with improved pharmacokinetic properties and potency compared to hexarelin and GHRP-6.

overview: Alexamorelin is a heptapeptide GH secretagogue that underwent early clinical evaluation but was not advanced to late-stage development. It belongs to the same pharmacological class as GHRP-6, GHRP-2, and hexarelin.
It was designed to have improved stability and potency compared to earlier hexapeptide GHRPs, with the addition of an N-terminal alanine residue.
Clinical development was limited and the compound remains primarily of research interest as part of the GHRP class.
researchContext: Early clinical studies demonstrated dose-dependent GH release in healthy volunteers, validating its activity at the GHSR.
Alexamorelin contributed to the pharmacological understanding of structure-activity relationships in growth hormone secretagogues.
It has been largely superseded by more selective agents like ipamorelin and non-peptide alternatives like MK-677.
safetyNotes: Limited clinical safety data available. The expected side effect profile is consistent with other GHSR agonists: potential hunger, flushing, and mild cortisol elevation.
As a research compound with minimal clinical development, human safety and efficacy data are insufficient for clinical use recommendations.

Structure-Activity Relationships of Growth Hormone Secretagogues

name: BPC-157
description: A pentadecapeptide derived from human gastric juice with broad tissue-repair and cytoprotective properties demonstrated in numerous preclinical studies.
mechanismOfAction: BPC-157 is a 15-amino acid peptide derived from a protective protein in human gastric juice. It promotes angiogenesis through upregulation of VEGF, eNOS, and EGF receptor expression. It modulates the NO system, interacts with the dopaminergic and serotonergic systems, and promotes tendon, ligament, muscle, and GI mucosal healing through multiple growth factor pathways. It also has demonstrated interaction with the FAK-paxillin pathway for cell migration and wound closure.

overview: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157) is a synthetic pentadecapeptide consisting of 15 amino acids derived from a larger protective protein found in human gastric juice. It was first described by Predrag Sikiric's research group at the University of Zagreb.

Preclinical research spanning over 30 years has demonstrated tissue-protective and healing effects across virtually every organ system studied, including tendon, muscle, bone, nerve, and gastrointestinal tissues.
BPC-157 is one of the most widely discussed peptides in the research and optimization community, valued for its broad healing applications despite the absence of completed human clinical trials.
researchContext: Hundreds of preclinical studies have demonstrated BPC-157's efficacy in healing tendon-to-bone injuries, muscle tears, nerve damage, skin wounds, GI ulcers, and inflammatory conditions in animal models.
The peptide is notable for its stability in gastric juice and efficacy via both systemic (injectable) and oral routes in animal models, which is unusual for a peptide.

While Diagen conducted an early-stage clinical trial for inflammatory bowel disease, comprehensive human clinical trial data remain unavailable, which limits evidence-based dosing and safety conclusions.
safetyNotes: No significant toxicity has been reported in extensive preclinical studies, even at doses far exceeding the typical experimental range. Rodent studies have not identified carcinogenic or mutagenic signals.
The absence of formal human clinical trial data means safety in humans is not rigorously established. The FDA issued warning letters to compounding pharmacies marketing BPC-157 in 2023-2024.

name: TB-500
description: A synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4 that promotes cell migration, angiogenesis, and tissue repair across multiple tissue types.
mechanismOfAction: TB-500 contains the active region of thymosin beta-4, a 43-amino acid actin-sequestering protein. It promotes cell migration by upregulating actin polymerization, enabling cells to move through tissue for repair. It stimulates angiogenesis, reduces inflammation by downregulating NF-κB and pro-inflammatory cytokines, and promotes stem cell differentiation and hair follicle growth. The central actin-binding motif LKKTET (Ac-SDKP after enzymatic cleavage) is critical for its biological activity.

overview: TB-500 is commonly used as an interchangeable term with thymosin beta-4 (Tβ4), though technically TB-500 refers to a synthetic version. Thymosin beta-4 is a naturally occurring 43-amino acid peptide present in nearly all human cells, where it serves as the primary intracellular actin-sequestering protein.
Its tissue repair properties span cardiac, dermal, corneal, neural, and musculoskeletal tissues. It promotes healing by enhancing cell migration, angiogenesis, and anti-inflammatory signaling.
TB-500 gained initial attention in equine veterinary medicine for treating racehorse injuries, and has since become widely used in the human peptide research community for injury recovery.
researchContext: Thymosin beta-4 has been studied in phase 2 clinical trials for cardiac repair after myocardial infarction, corneal wound healing (dry eye, neurotrophic keratitis), and epidermolysis bullosa.
RegeneRx Biopharmaceuticals developed RGN-259 (a thymosin beta-4 eye drop) for dry eye syndrome, with phase 3 trials showing improvements in corneal staining scores.
Preclinical cardiac studies showed that thymosin beta-4 administered after myocardial infarction activated cardiac progenitor cells and improved cardiac function in mouse models.
safetyNotes: Clinical trials of thymosin beta-4 formulations have reported favorable safety profiles. Systemic administration has not shown significant adverse events in published trials.
Theoretical concerns exist about promoting growth in pre-existing tumors due to angiogenic and cell migration effects, though clinical evidence for this risk has not been demonstrated.

name: Thymosin Alpha-1
description: A thymic peptide that modulates immune function by enhancing T-cell maturation, dendritic cell activity, and antiviral responses.
mechanismOfAction: Thymosin alpha-1 is a 28-amino acid peptide naturally produced by the thymus. It enhances T-cell differentiation and maturation (particularly Th1 responses), activates dendritic cells and natural killer cells, and promotes MHC class I antigen expression. It modulates toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling and increases production of interferon-alpha and IL-2, supporting antiviral and anti-tumor immunity without causing excessive inflammatory responses.

overview: Thymosin alpha-1 (thymalfasin) is a 28-amino acid peptide first isolated from thymosin fraction 5 by Allan Goldstein at George Washington University. It is the most clinically advanced thymic peptide.
Marketed as Zadaxin, it is approved in over 35 countries (not FDA-approved) as an immunomodulator for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and as an immune adjuvant in cancer treatment.
Its mechanism is immunomodulatory rather than immunosuppressive or broadly immunostimulatory: it enhances immune surveillance and pathogen-specific responses while promoting immune homeostasis.
researchContext: Clinical trials in chronic hepatitis B demonstrated improved virological response rates when thymosin alpha-1 was combined with interferon or used as monotherapy.
In oncology, thymosin alpha-1 has been studied as an adjuvant to chemotherapy and as a supportive agent for immune reconstitution in immunocompromised patients.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, thymosin alpha-1 was used in several countries as an adjunctive treatment for severe SARS-CoV-2 infection, with retrospective studies suggesting potential mortality benefits in critically ill patients.
safetyNotes: Thymosin alpha-1 has an excellent safety record across decades of clinical use internationally. Common side effects are limited to mild injection site reactions.
No significant immunosuppression, autoimmune exacerbation, or drug interactions have been reported in clinical studies.

name: KPV
description: A C-terminal tripeptide fragment of alpha-MSH with potent anti-inflammatory properties, studied for inflammatory bowel disease and skin conditions.
mechanismOfAction: KPV is the C-terminal tripeptide (Lys-Pro-Val, residues 11-13) of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). It retains the anti-inflammatory activity of the parent peptide while lacking melanocortin receptor binding activity. KPV inhibits NF-κB nuclear translocation and reduces production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β). It also inhibits inflammatory signaling through direct interaction with inflammatory cell pathways independently of melanocortin receptors.

overview: KPV is a naturally occurring tripeptide corresponding to the last three amino acids of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Despite its small size, it retains significant anti-inflammatory activity.
Unlike the full α-MSH peptide, KPV does not activate melanocortin receptors responsible for pigmentation changes, isolating the anti-inflammatory mechanism from melanogenic effects.
Its small size and oral stability make it an attractive candidate for oral and topical anti-inflammatory applications, particularly in gastrointestinal and dermatological contexts.
researchContext: Preclinical studies in mouse models of inflammatory bowel disease (colitis) showed that oral KPV reduced mucosal inflammation and improved disease activity scores.
Research has demonstrated that KPV can be delivered in nanoparticle formulations targeted to inflamed colonic tissue, enhancing local anti-inflammatory effects.
KPV has also shown efficacy in preclinical models of allergic contact dermatitis and wound healing, consistent with its anti-inflammatory mechanism.
safetyNotes: As an endogenous peptide fragment, KPV is expected to have a favorable safety profile. Preclinical studies have not identified significant toxicity.
Human clinical trial data are not available. Dosing, pharmacokinetics, and long-term safety in humans remain unestablished.

name: LL-37
description: A human cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide with broad-spectrum activity against bacteria, viruses, and fungi, plus immunomodulatory functions.
mechanismOfAction: LL-37 is the only human cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, cleaved from the precursor protein hCAP18 by proteinase 3. It forms an amphipathic alpha-helix that disrupts microbial membranes through electrostatic interaction with negatively charged lipid bilayers. Beyond direct antimicrobial activity, LL-37 modulates innate immunity by acting as a chemoattractant for immune cells, promoting angiogenesis, neutralizing LPS (endotoxin), and modulating toll-like receptor signaling.

overview: LL-37 is the sole member of the human cathelicidin family, a 37-amino acid peptide produced by neutrophils, macrophages, and epithelial cells at sites of infection and inflammation.

It has broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, enveloped viruses, and fungi. Its mechanism involves direct membrane disruption as well as immune cell recruitment.
Beyond antimicrobial defense, LL-37 plays important roles in wound healing, angiogenesis, and immune regulation, making it a multi-functional innate defense peptide.
researchContext: Research has explored LL-37 as a topical agent for wound infections, chronic wounds, and biofilm disruption. Its ability to disrupt bacterial biofilms is particularly valuable against antibiotic-resistant organisms.
Clinical trials have investigated LL-37 derivatives for conditions including leg ulcers and diabetic wound healing.
LL-37 dysregulation is associated with several diseases: deficiency increases infection susceptibility, while overexpression contributes to psoriasis and rosacea pathology.
safetyNotes: At high concentrations, LL-37 can exhibit cytotoxicity to mammalian cells due to its membrane-active mechanism. Therapeutic concentrations must balance antimicrobial efficacy with host cell compatibility.
Systemic administration carries theoretical risks of immune activation. Most research focuses on topical or localized delivery to minimize systemic exposure.

name: GHK
description: A naturally occurring tripeptide with tissue remodeling, anti-aging, and wound-healing properties, most commonly used as its copper complex GHK-Cu.
mechanismOfAction: GHK is a tripeptide (Gly-His-Lys) that has high affinity for copper(II) ions. It modulates gene expression related to tissue remodeling, activating genes involved in collagen synthesis, decorin production, and growth factor signaling while suppressing genes associated with inflammation and tissue destruction. GHK stimulates metalloproteinase activity for ECM remodeling and promotes fibroblast and keratinocyte migration for wound healing.

overview: GHK (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine) was first identified by Loren Pickart in 1973 as a factor in human plasma that caused aged liver tissue to synthesize proteins like younger tissue.
Circulating GHK levels decline significantly with age: from approximately 200 ng/mL at age 20 to 80 ng/mL by age 60, paralleling age-related decline in tissue repair capacity.
While GHK has intrinsic biological activity, it is most commonly used as its copper complex (GHK-Cu), which enhances copper delivery to tissues and may contribute additional biochemical effects.
researchContext: Gene expression studies (Broad Institute Connectivity Map) revealed that GHK modulates the expression of over 4,000 genes, with patterns consistent with tissue remodeling and reduced inflammation.
Research applications span wound healing, skin rejuvenation, hair growth stimulation, and potential systemic anti-aging effects based on gene expression modulation.
GHK and GHK-Cu are well-established in cosmeceutical formulations, with clinical evidence supporting improvements in skin firmness, elasticity, and wrinkle reduction.
safetyNotes: GHK has an excellent safety profile in topical applications and has been used in cosmetic products for decades without significant adverse events.
Systemic safety data are limited. Copper peptide products should be used at recommended concentrations as excessive copper can be cytotoxic.

name: GHK-Cu
description: The copper complex of the GHK tripeptide, widely used in skincare for collagen synthesis, wound healing, and anti-aging effects.
mechanismOfAction: GHK-Cu is the copper(II) complex of the GHK tripeptide, where copper is chelated by the glycine amine nitrogen, histidine imidazole nitrogen, and the deprotonated amide nitrogen. It delivers copper to tissues, which is essential for lysyl oxidase (collagen cross-linking), superoxide dismutase (antioxidant defense), and cytochrome c oxidase. The peptide component modulates over 4,000 genes involved in tissue remodeling, collagen synthesis, anti-inflammatory responses, and stem cell recruitment.

overview: GHK-Cu is the copper chelate form of the GHK tripeptide and is the most extensively studied and commercially used copper peptide in cosmeceuticals and wound care.
Clinical studies have demonstrated that GHK-Cu creams improve skin firmness, elasticity, and thickness while reducing fine lines, photodamage, and hyperpigmentation.
Beyond cosmetic applications, GHK-Cu has been studied for surgical wound healing, diabetic ulcers, and hair follicle stimulation, with positive results in controlled trials.
researchContext: Controlled studies showed that GHK-Cu facial cream significantly outperformed vitamin C and retinoic acid creams in improving collagen synthesis and reducing photoaging in a 12-week trial.
Hair growth studies demonstrated that GHK-Cu can increase hair follicle size and stimulate the growth phase (anagen), potentially comparable to minoxidil in early research.
The gene expression profile of GHK-Cu suggests anti-fibrotic, anti-inflammatory, and regenerative properties that extend beyond its cosmetic applications.
safetyNotes: GHK-Cu is very well tolerated topically and has been used safely in commercial skincare for over 20 years. Irritation is rare at standard concentrations.
Excessive copper exposure through very high concentrations or misuse could theoretically cause local toxicity. Systemic absorption from topical application is minimal.

name: AHK-Cu
description: A copper-binding tripeptide analogue of GHK-Cu developed for hair growth and skin rejuvenation applications.
mechanismOfAction: AHK-Cu is a copper-binding tripeptide (Ala-His-Lys) with structural similarity to GHK-Cu. It stimulates hair follicle proliferation by upregulating Wnt/β-catenin signaling and VEGF expression in dermal papilla cells. It promotes the anagen (growth) phase of the hair cycle and supports keratinocyte migration and proliferation for wound healing and skin renewal.

overview: AHK-Cu is a synthetic copper peptide analogue where the glycine residue of GHK is replaced with alanine. This modification was designed to optimize hair growth-stimulating properties.
Research has focused primarily on AHK-Cu's ability to stimulate dermal papilla cells and promote hair follicle growth through Wnt signaling activation.
It is used in cosmeceutical formulations targeting hair loss and scalp health, often alongside GHK-Cu in multi-peptide formulations.
researchContext: In vitro studies showed that AHK-Cu stimulated proliferation of human dermal papilla cells and upregulated VEGF and β-catenin expression, both critical for hair follicle development.
Comparative studies with GHK-Cu suggest that AHK-Cu may have enhanced specificity for hair growth pathways, though direct head-to-head clinical data are limited.
The peptide is primarily found in cosmeceutical research literature rather than pharmaceutical development.
safetyNotes: Topical safety data are consistent with other copper tripeptides: well tolerated at cosmeceutical concentrations with minimal irritation risk.
Clinical safety and efficacy data from controlled human trials are limited.

name: PT-141
description: A melanocortin receptor agonist approved for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal women, acting through central nervous system pathways.
mechanismOfAction: PT-141 (bremelanotide) is a cyclic heptapeptide that acts as an agonist at melanocortin-4 receptors (MC4R) in the central nervous system. Unlike PDE5 inhibitors that act peripherally on vascular smooth muscle, bremelanotide activates neural pathways in the hypothalamus and limbic system involved in sexual arousal and desire. It also has activity at MC1R and MC3R, which contribute to its side effect profile.

overview: PT-141 (bremelanotide) was approved by the FDA in 2019 under the brand name Vyleesi for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women. It is the first FDA-approved treatment that acts on central pathways of sexual desire.
It was originally derived from melanotan II through structure-activity optimization to enhance selectivity for sexual function-related melanocortin receptors.
Unlike other sexual dysfunction treatments, bremelanotide targets desire rather than arousal, representing a fundamentally different approach to sexual health pharmacology.
researchContext: The RECONNECT clinical trial program demonstrated that bremelanotide significantly increased sexual desire and reduced distress related to low desire in premenopausal women with HSDD.
Early research also explored bremelanotide for male erectile dysfunction, with positive results, though this indication was not pursued due to transient blood pressure elevation.
The melanocortin system's role in sexual function has been an active research area, with PT-141 providing the clinical proof-of-concept for central-acting sexual desire therapeutics.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include nausea (40% of patients), flushing, headache, and injection site reactions. Nausea is the most treatment-limiting adverse effect.
Bremelanotide causes transient increases in blood pressure and is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Use is limited to 8 doses per month.

Melanocortin Receptor Agonists in the Treatment of Sexual Dysfunction

name: Melanotan I
description: A synthetic α-MSH analogue developed for photoprotection that stimulates melanin production, also known as afamelanotide in its approved form.
mechanismOfAction: Melanotan I (afamelanotide) is a linear 13-amino acid synthetic analogue of α-MSH with substitutions at positions 4 (Nle) and 7 (D-Phe) that increase potency and metabolic stability. It activates MC1R on melanocytes, stimulating eumelanin production through the cAMP/PKA/MITF pathway. Eumelanin provides photoprotection by absorbing UV radiation and scavenging reactive oxygen species.

overview: Melanotan I was developed at the University of Arizona by Victor Hruby and Mac Hadley as a photoprotective agent. Its approved pharmaceutical form, afamelanotide (Scenesse), is marketed by Clinuvel Pharmaceuticals.
Afamelanotide received EMA approval in 2014 and FDA approval in 2019 for the prevention of phototoxicity in adults with erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP), a rare genetic photosensitivity disorder.
It is administered as a subcutaneous biodegradable implant that slowly releases the peptide over approximately 60 days, producing sustained melanin production.
researchContext: Clinical trials in EPP patients demonstrated significantly increased pain-free time in sunlight and improved quality of life with afamelanotide implant treatment.

Research is exploring afamelanotide for other photosensitivity conditions, vitiligo (in combination with narrowband UVB phototherapy), and potentially as a photoprotective agent for skin cancer prevention.
Melanotan I has a more favorable safety profile than Melanotan II due to its greater MC1R selectivity and lack of significant MC4R activity.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include nausea, headache, and implant site reactions. Skin darkening (the intended effect) may be cosmetically significant.
Long-term safety monitoring is ongoing. Theoretical concerns about melanocytic stimulation and melanoma risk have not been substantiated in clinical studies, but surveillance is recommended.

references.title: Afamelanotide and Narrowband UV-B Phototherapy for the Treatment of Vitiligo
Afamelanotide for Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

contentJson.hubNote: Melanotan I / afamelanotide is the only regulatory-approved melanocortin peptide on Peptide Daily, anchoring the photoprotection category.

name: Melanotan II
description: A cyclic melanocortin peptide that stimulates tanning, sexual arousal, and appetite suppression through broad melanocortin receptor activation.
mechanismOfAction: Melanotan II is a cyclic heptapeptide analogue of α-MSH that non-selectively activates melanocortin receptors MC1R through MC5R. MC1R activation stimulates melanogenesis (tanning). MC3R and MC4R activation in the hypothalamus suppresses appetite and enhances sexual arousal. MC4R activation also drives penile erection through central and peripheral autonomic pathways. The broad receptor profile produces multiple simultaneous effects.

overview: Melanotan II was developed at the University of Arizona alongside Melanotan I. Unlike the linear, MC1R-selective Melanotan I, MT-II is a cyclic peptide with broad melanocortin receptor activity.
Its multi-receptor activation produces simultaneous tanning (MC1R), appetite suppression (MC3R/MC4R), and sexual arousal enhancement (MC4R), making it a multi-functional but non-selective agent.
MT-II was the parent compound from which PT-141 (bremelanotide) was derived through structural optimization to isolate sexual function effects.
researchContext: Clinical studies confirmed MT-II produces significant skin darkening and spontaneous penile erections in men, validating the melanocortin system's role in both pigmentation and sexual function.
The non-selective receptor profile led researchers to develop more targeted derivatives: afamelanotide (MC1R-selective tanning) and bremelanotide (MC4R sexual function).
MT-II is widely available through unregulated online sources and is used for cosmetic tanning despite lack of regulatory approval, raising public health concerns.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include nausea (often at initial doses), facial flushing, fatigue, and spontaneous erections. Moles and nevi may darken, requiring dermatological monitoring.

Case reports have associated MT-II use with melanoma development in pre-existing moles, though a causal relationship has not been established. Users with many atypical nevi should exercise particular caution.

Synthetic α-Melanocyte Stimulating Hormone Analogue Melanotan II

name: Setmelanotide
description: A selective MC4R agonist approved for genetic obesity disorders caused by deficiencies in the leptin-melanocortin pathway.
mechanismOfAction: Setmelanotide is a cyclic octapeptide that selectively agonizes the melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) in the hypothalamus. In patients with genetic deficiencies in the leptin-melanocortin signaling pathway (POMC, PCSK1, or LEPR deficiency), MC4R is understimulated, leading to severe hyperphagia and early-onset obesity. Setmelanotide restores MC4R signaling downstream of the deficiency, reducing hunger and body weight.

overview: Setmelanotide was approved by the FDA in 2020 under the brand name Imcivree for chronic weight management in patients aged 6 years and older with monogenic obesity due to POMC, PCSK1, or LEPR deficiency confirmed by genetic testing.
It is the first precision medicine approach to obesity, targeting a specific molecular deficiency in the leptin-melanocortin signaling pathway rather than treating obesity as a single disease.

Clinical trials demonstrated dramatic weight loss (>10% body weight) and significant hunger reduction in genetically confirmed patients, effects that were not observed in patients without pathway deficiencies.
researchContext: The approval was expanded to include Bardet-Biedl syndrome in 2022, broadening the eligible genetic obesity population.
Research is exploring setmelanotide for additional genetic obesity syndromes affecting the melanocortin pathway, including POMC heterozygous deficiency and SH2B1 deficiency.
Setmelanotide's precision mechanism provides proof-of-concept for genotype-guided obesity treatment, contrasting with the broader-acting GLP-1 agonist approach.
safetyNotes: Common side effects include injection site reactions, skin hyperpigmentation (due to residual MC1R activity), and spontaneous penile erections in males. Depression and suicidal ideation are monitored.
Hyperpigmentation occurs in the majority of patients and is related to MC1R activation. Sexual adverse events related to MC4R activation require counseling.

references.title: Setmelanotide for POMC Deficiency Obesity
Setmelanotide for Bardet-Biedl Syndrome

name: Kisspeptin-10
description: The bioactive C-terminal decapeptide of kisspeptin that potently stimulates GnRH release, studied for reproductive endocrinology and IVF applications.
mechanismOfAction: Kisspeptin-10 is the C-terminal 10-amino acid fragment of the 54-amino acid kisspeptin protein (encoded by the KISS1 gene). It binds to the kisspeptin receptor (KISS1R/GPR54) on GnRH neurons in the hypothalamus, potently stimulating the release of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH). This triggers downstream luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) release from the pituitary, making it a key regulator of the reproductive axis.

overview: Kisspeptin-10 is the minimal bioactive fragment of the kisspeptin family, discovered through research on the KISS1 gene's role as a metastasis suppressor. Its role as a master regulator of reproductive function was elucidated when loss-of-function mutations in KISS1R were found to cause hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
It is one of the most potent known stimulators of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, capable of triggering LH release at low doses in both men and women.
Clinical research is focused on using kisspeptin as a safer alternative to hCG for triggering oocyte maturation in IVF, potentially reducing the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS).
researchContext: Clinical trials at Imperial College London and Hammersmith Hospital have demonstrated that kisspeptin-54 can safely trigger oocyte maturation in IVF cycles with reduced OHSS risk compared to hCG.
Research in men has shown that kisspeptin administration can be used diagnostically to assess hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis function and as a potential treatment for hypogonadism.
Kisspeptin's role in puberty initiation, reproductive seasonality, and metabolic-reproductive integration continues to be an active area of neuroendocrine research.
safetyNotes: Kisspeptin has shown a favorable safety profile in clinical studies. Unlike GnRH agonists, kisspeptin does not cause pituitary desensitization with short-term use.
Adverse effects in clinical trials have been minimal, primarily limited to injection site discomfort. Long-term effects of repeated kisspeptin administration are not fully characterized.

Kisspeptin and the Control of Gonadotropin Secretion in Humans

name: Semax
description: A synthetic heptapeptide analogue of ACTH(4-10) developed in Russia with nootropic, neuroprotective, and neurorestorative properties.
mechanismOfAction: Semax is a synthetic heptapeptide consisting of ACTH(4-10) with a C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro tripeptide addition for metabolic stability. It enhances BDNF and TrkB expression, modulates serotonergic and dopaminergic neurotransmission, and promotes neuronal survival through activation of PI3K/Akt and MAPK pathways. It does not have the adrenocortical activity of full-length ACTH, as the melanocortin receptor binding domain is truncated.

overview: Semax was developed at the Institute of Molecular Genetics of the Russian Academy of Sciences and is approved in Russia and several CIS countries as a nootropic and neuroprotective agent.

It is prescribed in Russia for stroke recovery, cognitive enhancement, optic nerve disease, and stress-related conditions. The standard formulation is a 0.1% intranasal solution; a 1% formulation is used for neurological conditions.
Semax is one of the best-characterized nootropic peptides, with a substantial body of Russian-language literature and growing international research interest.
researchContext: Clinical studies in Russia demonstrated cognitive improvement in patients with dyscirculatory encephalopathy, post-stroke recovery, and attention deficit disorders.
Preclinical research has shown that Semax increases BDNF levels in the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex, promotes neurogenesis, and protects neurons from ischemic and excitotoxic damage.
The Pro-Gly-Pro C-terminal extension is itself biologically active (as a fragment of collagen) and may contribute additional anti-inflammatory effects.
safetyNotes: Semax has a favorable safety profile based on decades of clinical use in Russia. No significant adverse effects have been reported at standard intranasal doses.
It does not produce steroidogenic effects, addiction potential, or withdrawal symptoms. However, Western regulatory-quality clinical trial data are limited.

references.title: Semax, an Analogue of ACTH(4-10), Regulates Expression of Immune Response Genes During Ischemic Brain Injury in Rats
Neuroprotective Effects of Semax: A Review

name: Selank
description: A synthetic heptapeptide analogue of the immunopeptide tuftsin with anxiolytic, nootropic, and immunomodulatory properties.
mechanismOfAction: Selank is a synthetic analogue of the endogenous tetrapeptide tuftsin (Thr-Lys-Pro-Arg) with a C-terminal Pro-Gly-Pro addition for stability. It modulates GABAergic neurotransmission (enhancing GABA receptor sensitivity), increases BDNF expression, and influences serotonin metabolism by inhibiting enkephalin-degrading enzymes. Its immunomodulatory effects include enhanced IL-6, monocyte chemotactic protein expression, and modulation of T-helper cell balance.

overview: Selank was developed alongside Semax at the Institute of Molecular Genetics, Russian Academy of Sciences. It is approved in Russia as an anxiolytic and nootropic agent.
While Semax is based on ACTH, Selank is based on tuftsin, an endogenous immunopeptide. The Pro-Gly-Pro stabilizing tripeptide is shared with Semax.
Its combined anxiolytic and nootropic profile — reducing anxiety while enhancing cognitive function — makes it distinct from traditional benzodiazepine anxiolytics that typically impair cognition.
researchContext: Clinical studies in Russia demonstrated anxiolytic efficacy comparable to benzodiazepines but without sedation, cognitive impairment, or dependence potential.
Gene expression studies showed that Selank influences over 50 genes related to GABAergic signaling, inflammation, and neuroprotection in the hippocampus.
The immunomodulatory component distinguishes Selank from pure anxiolytics: it may provide dual benefit in conditions where anxiety and immune dysfunction coexist.
safetyNotes: Selank has a favorable safety profile based on Russian clinical use. It does not produce sedation, muscle relaxation, or dependence typical of GABAergic anxiolytics.
No significant adverse effects have been reported at standard doses. As with Semax, international regulatory-quality clinical data are limited.

name: Dihexa
description: A hexapeptide derivative of angiotensin IV with potent procognitive effects, capable of crossing the blood-brain barrier.
mechanismOfAction: Dihexa is a modified dipeptide derivative of angiotensin IV that acts as a potent agonist of hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)/c-Met receptor signaling. It augments HGF/c-Met dimerization and activates downstream PI3K/Akt and MAPK pathways, promoting synaptogenesis, dendritic spine formation, and neuronal survival. In preclinical models, it demonstrated 10-million-fold greater potency than BDNF in promoting neurite outgrowth.

overview: Dihexa was developed by Joseph Harding and colleagues at Washington State University as part of research on angiotensin IV receptor-mediated cognitive enhancement.
It is one of the most potent procognitive agents discovered, demonstrating the ability to reverse scopolamine-induced cognitive deficits in rats at picomolar concentrations.
Its oral bioavailability and blood-brain barrier penetration make it pharmacologically accessible, though it remains a research compound without clinical trials.
researchContext: Preclinical studies demonstrated that Dihexa rescued cognitive deficits in aged rats and reversed scopolamine-induced memory impairment, with efficacy comparable to or exceeding other nootropic agents at far lower doses.
The HGF/c-Met mechanism is distinct from traditional neurotransmitter-modulating nootropics, targeting synaptic plasticity and neuronal connectivity rather than acute neurotransmission.
A patent was filed for Dihexa's use in Alzheimer's disease, but pharmaceutical development has not progressed to clinical trials.
safetyNotes: No human safety data are available. Preclinical toxicology data are limited. The HGF/c-Met pathway is also involved in tumor cell migration and metastasis, raising theoretical oncological concerns.
The extreme potency of Dihexa means dosing errors could produce outsized effects. No established safe dosing range for humans exists.
