name: NA-Selank
description: An acetylated form of Selank with enhanced metabolic stability and potentially improved bioavailability compared to standard Selank.
mechanismOfAction: NA-Selank is the N-acetylated form of Selank, where an acetyl group is added to the N-terminus (threonine) of the peptide. This modification protects against aminopeptidase degradation, extending the peptide's half-life and potentially improving its pharmacokinetic profile. The mechanism of action is otherwise consistent with Selank: modulation of GABAergic neurotransmission, BDNF upregulation, and immune modulation.

overview: NA-Selank is a structural modification of Selank designed to improve metabolic stability through N-terminal acetylation, a common pharmaceutical chemistry approach.
The acetylation protects the peptide from exopeptidase degradation, which is the primary clearance mechanism for small peptides after intranasal administration.
It retains the anxiolytic, nootropic, and immunomodulatory properties of Selank with potentially improved duration of action.
researchContext: Published research on NA-Selank specifically (as opposed to Selank) is limited. Most claims are based on extrapolation from Selank data combined with general knowledge of N-acetylation effects on peptide stability.
N-acetylation is a well-established technique in peptide pharmacology for improving resistance to enzymatic degradation while generally preserving receptor binding.
The modification has gained popularity in the peptide optimization community but lacks independent clinical validation.
safetyNotes: Safety is expected to parallel standard Selank, with the acetyl modification being a common and generally benign pharmaceutical modification.
No independent safety or efficacy data for NA-Selank exist in peer-reviewed literature.

name: NA-Semax
description: An acetylated amidate form of Semax with enhanced stability and potency, designed for improved intranasal delivery.
mechanismOfAction: NA-Semax is the N-acetylated and C-amidated form of Semax, providing dual-end protection against exopeptidase degradation. The mechanism of action is consistent with Semax: BDNF upregulation, TrkB activation, dopaminergic and serotonergic modulation, and neuroprotection through PI3K/Akt and MAPK pathways. The structural modifications increase metabolic stability and may enhance blood-brain barrier penetration.

overview: NA-Semax (N-Acetyl Semax Amidate) represents a doubly modified version of Semax with both N-terminal acetylation and C-terminal amidation for maximum enzymatic stability.
These modifications are standard pharmaceutical chemistry techniques that protect against aminopeptidase (N-terminal) and carboxypeptidase (C-terminal) degradation.
Users in the nootropics community report enhanced potency and duration compared to standard Semax, though controlled comparative data are not available.
researchContext: Published research on NA-Semax specifically is limited; most evidence is extrapolated from the extensive Semax literature combined with known effects of terminal modifications on peptide stability.
The dual modification approach is pharmacologically sound and is used in many approved peptide drugs to improve their pharmacokinetic profiles.
Comparative pharmacokinetic studies between Semax and NA-Semax in humans have not been published.
safetyNotes: Safety is expected to parallel standard Semax. The acetylation and amidation modifications are generally non-toxic and widely used in pharmaceutical peptide chemistry.
No independent clinical safety data for NA-Semax exist.

name: P21
description: A synthetic peptide derived from the active region of Cerebrolysin's CNTF-like activity, designed to promote neurogenesis and cognitive function.
mechanismOfAction: P21 is a synthetic tetrapeptide amide (Ac-DGGL-NH2) designed to mimic the neurotrophic effects of ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF) without the mitogenic (cell proliferation) side effects. It inhibits leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) signaling while promoting neurogenesis and dendritic arborization through CNTF receptor-mediated pathways. It reduces tau hyperphosphorylation and promotes synaptic plasticity in preclinical models.

overview: P21 was developed by Khalid Iqbal's research group at the New York State Institute for Basic Research in Developmental Disabilities as a synthetic neurogenic compound.

It was designed to replicate the beneficial neurogenic and neurotrophic effects observed with the biologic Cerebrolysin while offering a defined synthetic molecule with potentially superior pharmacological properties.
Preclinical studies demonstrated that P21 promoted neurogenesis, reduced tau pathology, and improved cognitive function in transgenic Alzheimer's disease mouse models.
researchContext: Studies in 3xTg-AD mice showed that P21 rescued cognitive deficits, increased hippocampal neurogenesis, and reduced both amyloid and tau pathology markers.
The compound's ability to promote neurogenesis without mitogenic activity is important for safety, as uncontrolled cell proliferation is a concern with many growth factors.
P21 has not entered formal clinical development. It remains a preclinical research tool with interest in the nootropics community.
safetyNotes: Preclinical studies have not identified significant toxicity. The design specifically aimed to eliminate mitogenic effects that could promote tumor growth.
No human safety data exist. As a research compound, appropriate dosing and safety parameters for human use are not established.

references.title: A Small Peptide P21 Rescues Cognition, Tau and Amyloid Pathology in 3xTg-AD Mice
P21, a Neurogenic CNTF Peptide, Promotes Neurogenesis and Improves Memory Impairment in AD Mice

name: Cerebrolysin
description: A mixture of neurotrophic peptides and amino acids derived from porcine brain tissue, approved in multiple countries for neurological disorders.
mechanismOfAction: Cerebrolysin is a standardized mixture of low-molecular-weight neuropeptides (<10 kDa) and free amino acids derived from porcine brain proteins via biotechnological processing. The peptide fraction mimics the effects of endogenous neurotrophic factors including BDNF, GDNF, NGF, and CNTF. It promotes neuronal survival, synaptic plasticity, neurogenesis, and oligodendrogenesis through multiple neurotrophic signaling pathways.

overview: Cerebrolysin is a biologic neuropeptide preparation manufactured by EVER Pharma (formerly Ebewe Pharma) and approved in over 40 countries for stroke, traumatic brain injury, and dementia, though it is not FDA-approved.
It contains a defined mixture of brain-derived peptides and amino acids that collectively reproduce neurotrophic factor activity, promoting neuronal survival and synaptic plasticity.
Clinical experience spans over 45 years with extensive use in European and Asian countries for various neurological conditions.
researchContext: The CASTA stroke trial and subsequent meta-analyses showed modest benefits in functional recovery after ischemic stroke when Cerebrolysin was administered early.
In Alzheimer's disease, clinical trials demonstrated improvements in cognitive function scores (ADAS-cog), with benefits persisting for several months after treatment cessation.
A Cochrane review noted that while individual trials showed positive trends, the overall evidence quality was moderate and larger confirmatory trials are needed.
safetyNotes: Cerebrolysin has a well-documented safety profile from decades of clinical use. Common side effects include dizziness, headache, and injection site reactions.
As a porcine-derived biologic, allergic reactions are possible though rare. It should not be used in patients with status epilepticus or severe renal impairment.

name: DSIP
description: A naturally occurring nonapeptide involved in sleep regulation, stress response modulation, and neuroendocrine function.
mechanismOfAction: DSIP is a nonapeptide first isolated from rabbit cerebral venous blood during electrically induced sleep. Its mechanism remains incompletely characterized, but it appears to modulate sleep architecture through interactions with the serotonergic system and modification of delta wave (slow-wave sleep) generation. It also modulates corticotropin and cortisol levels, influences LH release, and may interact with opioid receptor systems. It crosses the blood-brain barrier via a saturable transport system.

overview: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide) was first described by Schoenenberger and Monnier in 1977 after its isolation from the cerebral venous blood of rabbits during induced slow-wave sleep.

Despite its name, DSIP's effects on sleep are complex and not simply soporific: it appears to normalize disturbed sleep patterns rather than universally inducing sleep, and it modulates circadian rhythm and stress responses.
Its short plasma half-life and rapid enzymatic degradation have limited pharmaceutical development, though analogues with improved stability have been explored.
researchContext: Clinical studies in insomnia patients showed improvements in sleep onset latency, sleep quality, and morning alertness, with effects more pronounced in patients with disrupted sleep than in normal sleepers.
Research has demonstrated stress-protective properties: DSIP administration reduced stress-induced metabolic and hormonal disturbances in animal models.
DSIP has been studied as a potential treatment for opioid and alcohol withdrawal, with small clinical studies showing reduced withdrawal symptoms.
safetyNotes: Limited clinical data suggest a favorable safety profile with no significant adverse effects at research doses. DSIP does not produce the cognitive impairment or dependency of benzodiazepine hypnotics.
The very short half-life and lack of standardized pharmaceutical formulations limit practical clinical utility.

name: Noopept
description: A synthetic dipeptide derivative with nootropic and neuroprotective properties, structurally related to racetams but with greater potency.
mechanismOfAction: Noopept is a synthetic dipeptide (N-phenylacetyl-L-prolylglycine ethyl ester) that is metabolized to cycloprolylglycine, an endogenous neuropeptide. It enhances BDNF and NGF expression in the hippocampus and cortex, modulates AMPA and NMDA receptor function to promote long-term potentiation (LTP), and provides neuroprotection through antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. It acts at 1000x lower doses than piracetam.

overview: Noopept (omberacetam) was developed at the Zakusov State Institute of Pharmacology in Russia and is approved in Russia as a nootropic for cognitive impairment of various origins.
Although often grouped with racetams, Noopept is structurally a dipeptide derivative rather than a pyrrolidone derivative. Its active metabolite, cycloprolylglycine, is an endogenous neuropeptide.
It is significantly more potent than piracetam on a per-milligram basis, with effective oral doses in the 10-30 mg range compared to piracetam's 1200-4800 mg.
researchContext: Clinical studies in Russian populations with mild cognitive impairment showed improvements in memory, attention, and cognitive processing speed.
Preclinical research demonstrated neuroprotective effects against amyloid-beta toxicity, oxidative stress, and ischemic damage, suggesting potential applications in neurodegenerative disease.
The compound's metabolite cycloprolylglycine has independent biological activity, including modulation of AMPA receptor sensitivity and endogenous anxiolytic effects.
safetyNotes: Noopept is well tolerated at standard doses in clinical studies. Side effects are rare and may include headache, restlessness, or sleep disturbances at higher doses.
It does not produce the stimulant-type side effects of amphetamines and has no demonstrated addiction potential.

name: FGL
description: A 15-amino acid peptide derived from neural cell adhesion molecule (NCAM) that enhances synaptic plasticity and neuroprotection through FGFR activation.
mechanismOfAction: FGL is a 15-amino acid synthetic peptide corresponding to a region of the second fibronectin type III module of neural cell adhesion molecule (NCAM) that interacts with fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR). It binds to FGFR1 and activates downstream PLCγ, MAPK, and PI3K/Akt signaling pathways, promoting synaptic plasticity, neurite outgrowth, and neuronal survival without the mitogenic risks of full-length FGF.

overview: FGL (FG Loop peptide) was identified through structural analysis of the NCAM-FGFR interaction interface. NCAM is a major cell adhesion molecule in the nervous system that promotes neurite outgrowth and synaptic plasticity partly through FGFR activation.
The peptide isolates the FGFR-binding domain of NCAM, providing targeted neurotrophic stimulation through a well-characterized receptor pathway.
Preclinical studies have shown that FGL enhances memory in aged rats and provides neuroprotection in models of neurodegeneration.
researchContext: Animal studies demonstrated that FGL improved spatial memory in aged rats and protected against memory deficits induced by amyloid-beta and other neurotoxic challenges.
A phase 1 clinical trial (ENKAM Pharmaceuticals) evaluated FGL safety in healthy volunteers, confirming tolerability.
The NCAM-FGFR signaling axis is important for learning and memory, and FGL provides a pharmacological tool to selectively engage this pathway.
safetyNotes: Phase 1 data showed acceptable safety in healthy volunteers. The peptide's selectivity for FGFR reduces theoretical concerns about broad growth factor stimulation.
Long-term safety data and efficacy in clinical populations are not available.

name: Matrixyl
description: A lipopeptide that stimulates collagen and glycosaminoglycan synthesis, one of the most clinically validated anti-wrinkle peptides in cosmetics.
mechanismOfAction: Matrixyl (palmitoyl pentapeptide-4) is a lipopeptide consisting of the pentapeptide KTTKS conjugated to a palmitic acid chain for skin penetration. KTTKS is a matrikine — a peptide fragment derived from the procollagen I C-terminal propeptide that signals collagen breakdown, triggering compensatory collagen synthesis. It activates TGF-β signaling and upregulates expression of collagens I, III, and IV, fibronectin, and glycosaminoglycans in dermal fibroblasts.

overview: Matrixyl was developed by Sederma and is one of the most commercially successful cosmeceutical peptides worldwide. The KTTKS sequence was identified as a collagen-synthesis-stimulating fragment from the procollagen I C-propeptide.
Clinical studies demonstrated visible reduction in wrinkle depth and volume comparable to retinol, with superior tolerability and no photosensitization.
The palmitic acid lipid tail enables skin penetration through the stratum corneum, which is the primary barrier to peptide delivery in topical formulations.
researchContext: Double-blind, placebo-controlled studies showed that 12 weeks of Matrixyl application significantly reduced wrinkle depth, surface area, and volume compared to placebo in periorbital skin.
In vitro studies demonstrated that Matrixyl stimulated collagen I synthesis by 117% and fibronectin synthesis by 327% in human dermal fibroblast cultures.
Second-generation products (Matrixyl 3000, Matrixyl Synthe'6) combine palmitoyl pentapeptide-4 with additional peptides for enhanced anti-aging effects.
safetyNotes: Matrixyl has an excellent safety profile in topical use. It does not cause photosensitization, irritation, or the retinoid-like side effects of retinol.
It is well tolerated across skin types and is suitable for sensitive skin formulations.

name: Argireline
description: A hexapeptide that mimics the N-terminal end of SNAP-25 to reduce neuromuscular junction activity, marketed as a topical anti-wrinkle agent.
mechanismOfAction: Argireline (acetyl hexapeptide-3) is a synthetic peptide that mimics the N-terminal portion of SNAP-25 (synaptosomal-associated protein of 25 kDa). It competes with native SNAP-25 for incorporation into the SNARE complex (SNAP-25/syntaxin/VAMP), inhibiting SNARE complex formation and reducing acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. This decreases the intensity of muscle contraction, reducing expression lines in a mechanism analogous to botulinum toxin but with much lower potency.

overview: Argireline was developed by Lipotec (now part of Lubrizol) and is marketed as a topical alternative to botulinum toxin injections for expression lines and wrinkles.
Its mechanism targets the same SNARE complex that botulinum toxin cleaves, but through competitive inhibition rather than enzymatic cleavage, producing a reversible and much milder effect.
It is one of the most widely used peptides in commercial anti-aging skincare, found in numerous over-the-counter products.
researchContext: Clinical studies showed that Argireline at 10% concentration reduced wrinkle depth by approximately 30% over 30 days in the periorbital area.
In vitro studies confirmed inhibition of catecholamine release from chromaffin cells, validating the SNARE complex inhibition mechanism.
Comparative studies with botulinum toxin suggest that while Argireline produces measurable wrinkle reduction, the magnitude is significantly less than injectable neurotoxin treatments.
safetyNotes: Argireline has an excellent topical safety profile with no reported systemic effects. Its poor skin penetration actually contributes to safety, as significant neuromuscular blockade would be undesirable.
No allergic reactions or irritation have been reported at standard cosmetic concentrations.

name: Leuphasyl
description: A pentapeptide that works synergistically with Argireline to reduce expression lines by modulating enkephalin pathways.
mechanismOfAction: Leuphasyl is a synthetic pentapeptide that mimics the activity of enkephalins at the neuromuscular junction. It modulates calcium-dependent acetylcholine release by interacting with opioid-like receptors on presynaptic nerve terminals, reducing neurotransmitter release and thereby decreasing muscle contraction intensity. When combined with Argireline (SNARE complex inhibitor), the two peptides act through complementary mechanisms to enhance wrinkle reduction.

overview: Leuphasyl (pentapeptide-18) is a cosmeceutical peptide developed as a complement to Argireline. Its sequence (Tyr-Ala-Gly-Phe-Leu) mimics the enkephalin peptide structure.
While Argireline targets the SNARE complex post-synaptically, Leuphasyl acts pre-synaptically to reduce neurotransmitter release, providing a dual-mechanism approach to expression line reduction.
Synergistic studies showed that combining Leuphasyl with Argireline produced greater wrinkle reduction than either peptide alone.
researchContext: In vitro studies demonstrated that Leuphasyl reduced catecholamine release from neuronal cells through an opioid receptor-mediated mechanism.
Combination studies with Argireline showed approximately 25% greater wrinkle reduction compared to Argireline alone over 28 days.
The enkephalin-mimetic mechanism represents a unique approach in cosmeceutical peptide design, targeting presynaptic neurotransmitter regulation.
safetyNotes: Leuphasyl has a favorable topical safety profile. No systemic opioid effects are expected due to the peptide's limited skin penetration and local mechanism of action.
No significant adverse events have been reported in cosmetic use.

name: SNAP-8
description: An octapeptide extension of Argireline with enhanced SNARE complex inhibition for deeper expression line reduction.
mechanismOfAction: SNAP-8 (acetyl octapeptide-3) is an elongated version of Argireline, containing eight amino acids mimicking the N-terminal sequence of SNAP-25. The additional two residues provide improved competitive inhibition of the SNARE complex (SNAP-25/syntaxin/VAMP) compared to the hexapeptide, more effectively blocking vesicular neurotransmitter fusion and acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction.

overview: SNAP-8 was developed by Lipotec as a next-generation version of Argireline, extending the SNAP-25-mimicking peptide from six to eight amino acids for improved SNARE complex inhibition.
The additional residues provide a better structural fit within the SNARE complex binding site, theoretically producing more effective competition with native SNAP-25.
It is marketed as providing deeper and faster wrinkle reduction compared to Argireline, particularly for deep expression lines.
researchContext: In vitro studies showed that SNAP-8 inhibited neurotransmitter release more effectively than Argireline at equivalent concentrations.
Clinical studies demonstrated wrinkle depth reduction of up to 35% over 28 days with SNAP-8 at 3% concentration.
Head-to-head comparisons with Argireline showed slightly superior efficacy for SNAP-8, supporting the structure-activity improvement.
safetyNotes: SNAP-8 shares Argireline's excellent topical safety profile. No adverse events beyond those of the vehicle have been reported.
The peptide's limited skin penetration ensures that neuromuscular effects remain localized to the superficial dermis.

name: Syn-Ake
description: A synthetic tripeptide mimicking the Waglerin-1 snake venom peptide, targeting nicotinic acetylcholine receptors to reduce expression wrinkles.
mechanismOfAction: Syn-Ake is a synthetic tripeptide designed to mimic the activity of Waglerin-1, a peptide from the venom of the Temple Viper (Tropidolaemus wagleri). It acts as a reversible antagonist at the muscular nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR), reducing receptor-mediated muscle contraction. This produces a localized muscle-relaxing effect when applied topically, smoothing expression lines through a mechanism distinct from SNARE complex inhibitors like Argireline.

overview: Syn-Ake was developed by DSM (formerly Pentapharm) as a biomimetic cosmeceutical inspired by snake venom peptides. It is a simplified synthetic tripeptide, not actual venom.
Its mechanism targets the postsynaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, complementing the presynaptic/SNARE complex mechanisms of Argireline, Leuphasyl, and SNAP-8.
Clinical studies showed rapid wrinkle reduction, with measurable improvements in expression line depth within 28 days of twice-daily application.
researchContext: In vitro studies confirmed that Syn-Ake inhibited acetylcholine-induced muscle contraction in cell-based assays.
Clinical studies demonstrated reduction in forehead wrinkle depth of approximately 52% over 28 days, though this figure comes from manufacturer-sponsored research.
The snake venom-mimetic approach has inspired additional cosmeceutical peptides targeting other venom-derived mechanisms.
safetyNotes: Syn-Ake is well tolerated topically. The synthetic tripeptide does not contain actual venom components and has no systemic neurotoxic potential.
Standard cosmeceutical safety testing has shown no irritation, sensitization, or phototoxicity.

name: Palmitoyl Tetrapeptide-7
description: A lipopeptide that reduces inflammation-driven skin aging by inhibiting IL-6 release, often combined with Matrixyl in anti-aging formulations.
mechanismOfAction: Palmitoyl tetrapeptide-7 is a lipopeptide that inhibits the production of interleukin-6 (IL-6) and other pro-inflammatory cytokines from keratinocytes and fibroblasts. By reducing inflammaging — chronic low-grade inflammation that accelerates skin aging — it decreases MMP expression and preserves extracellular matrix integrity. The palmitic acid moiety enables skin penetration through the lipid-rich stratum corneum.

overview: Palmitoyl tetrapeptide-7 (formerly known as Rigin) targets the inflammatory component of skin aging, complementing collagen-stimulating peptides like Matrixyl that target structural repair.
It is a key component of Matrixyl 3000 (Sederma), which combines palmitoyl tetrapeptide-7 with palmitoyl oligopeptide for dual anti-aging action.
By inhibiting IL-6, it addresses the 'inflammaging' theory — the concept that chronic low-level inflammation is a major driver of age-related tissue damage.
researchContext: In vitro studies showed significant inhibition of IL-6 release after UV exposure when fibroblasts were treated with palmitoyl tetrapeptide-7.
The Matrixyl 3000 combination product has clinical data showing synergistic wrinkle reduction beyond either component alone.
The anti-inflammatory mechanism distinguishes it from signal peptides (Matrixyl) and neurotransmitter-modulating peptides (Argireline) in the cosmeceutical landscape.
safetyNotes: Well tolerated topically with no reported adverse events. Suitable for sensitive and reactive skin types.
No systemic immunosuppressive effects are expected from topical application.

name: Thymulin
description: A zinc-dependent nonapeptide secreted by the thymus that modulates T-cell differentiation and immune function.
mechanismOfAction: Thymulin is a zinc-dependent nonapeptide produced exclusively by thymic epithelial cells. In its active zinc-bound form, it promotes T-cell differentiation and maturation, modulates cytokine production, and enhances NK cell activity. It requires zinc for biological activity — the zinc ion coordinates with the Asn, Ser, and the C-terminal region. Thymulin levels decline with age-related thymic involution, paralleling immune senescence.

overview: Thymulin (formerly called facteur thymique sérique or FTS) was identified by Jean-François Bach and colleagues at Hôpital Necker in Paris. It is the only thymic hormone for which the zinc-dependent structure is fully characterized.
It is exclusively produced by thymic epithelial cells, making it a specific marker of thymic function. Its levels decline dramatically after puberty as the thymus involutes.
Research interest focuses on thymulin's potential for immune reconstitution in aging and its role as a biomarker of thymic function.
researchContext: Studies have shown that zinc supplementation can partially restore thymulin activity in elderly and zinc-deficient populations, improving immune function.
Gene therapy approaches using thymulin gene expression have shown anti-inflammatory effects in animal models of autoimmune disease and aging.
Research on thymic rejuvenation strategies often includes thymulin as a functional endpoint of restored thymic activity.
safetyNotes: As an endogenous thymic peptide, thymulin is expected to have good biocompatibility. Clinical safety data from exogenous administration are limited.
Zinc supplementation to support thymulin activity should remain within recommended dietary limits to avoid copper depletion.

name: Thymalin
description: A thymic peptide extract containing multiple bioactive peptides, developed in Russia for immune reconstitution and studied in longevity research.
mechanismOfAction: Thymalin is a polypeptide complex extracted from calf thymus glands, containing multiple low-molecular-weight peptides. It modulates immune function by promoting T-lymphocyte differentiation, restoring the T-helper/T-suppressor cell ratio, enhancing phagocytic activity, and stimulating production of thymic hormones. Its multi-peptide composition acts on multiple immune regulatory pathways simultaneously.

overview: Thymalin was developed by Vladimir Khavinson at the Saint Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology as part of his bioregulatory peptide research program. It is approved in Russia as an immunomodulatory agent.
It is a thymus extract rather than a single defined peptide, containing multiple bioactive peptides that collectively restore immune function in immunocompromised patients.
Khavinson's research group has conducted long-term studies suggesting that thymic peptide supplementation may improve survival and reduce age-related diseases in elderly populations.
researchContext: A notable 6-year study by Khavinson and colleagues showed that combined thymalin and epithalon treatment in elderly patients (60-74 years) reduced cardiovascular mortality and improved immune parameters compared to controls.
The bioregulatory peptide concept posits that tissue-specific short peptides regulate gene expression in their tissues of origin, a framework central to Russian gerontology research.
Western validation of Khavinson's longevity claims remains limited, and the multi-peptide nature of thymalin makes precise mechanism characterization challenging.
safetyNotes: Thymalin has been used clinically in Russia for decades with a favorable safety profile. Reported side effects are rare and mild.
As a bovine-derived biologic, theoretical risks include allergic reactions and prion concerns, though no such events have been documented.

name: Larazotide
description: A synthetic peptide that tightens intestinal tight junctions, in clinical development for celiac disease to reduce gluten-triggered permeability.
mechanismOfAction: Larazotide acetate is an octapeptide derived from Vibrio cholerae zonula occludens toxin (Zot). It acts as a tight junction regulator by blocking the zonulin receptor pathway that gliadin peptides (from gluten) use to open intestinal tight junctions. By preventing paracellular permeability increases, it reduces the passage of immunogenic gluten fragments through the intestinal epithelium, thereby decreasing the immune response that causes celiac disease symptoms.

overview: Larazotide acetate was developed from research by Alessio Fasano on the zonulin pathway — a physiological mechanism regulating intestinal permeability. It is the first-in-class tight junction regulator.
It is designed as an adjunctive therapy for celiac disease, not as a replacement for gluten-free diet but as protection against inadvertent gluten exposure.
Phase 2 and phase 3 clinical trials have evaluated larazotide for symptom reduction in celiac patients on a gluten-free diet who continue to experience symptoms.
researchContext: Phase 2 trials showed that larazotide acetate (0.5 mg three times daily) significantly reduced symptoms and intestinal permeability in celiac patients exposed to gluten challenge.
The phase 3 trial assessed larazotide for symptomatic celiac disease in patients on a gluten-free diet. The primary endpoint was reduction of celiac disease symptom scores.
Beyond celiac disease, the tight junction regulatory mechanism has potential applications in other conditions characterized by increased intestinal permeability.
safetyNotes: Larazotide acts locally in the GI tract and is not systemically absorbed, which contributes to its favorable safety profile. Adverse events in clinical trials were comparable to placebo.
The most common side effects were GI-related: nausea, headache, and abdominal pain, occurring at similar rates in treatment and placebo groups.

name: Epitalon
description: A synthetic tetrapeptide based on the natural pineal peptide epithalamin, studied for telomerase activation and anti-aging properties.
mechanismOfAction: Epitalon (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) is a synthetic tetrapeptide developed to reproduce the effects of the natural pineal peptide extract epithalamin. It activates telomerase (hTERT) expression in human somatic cells, potentially extending replicative lifespan. It also stimulates melatonin production in the pineal gland, regulates neuroendocrine function, and modulates antioxidant enzyme expression. The telomerase activation mechanism appears to involve peptide regulation of gene expression in pineal and other tissues.

overview: Epitalon was developed by Vladimir Khavinson at the Saint Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology as a synthetic analogue of the pineal gland peptide extract epithalamin.
In vitro studies demonstrated that Epitalon activated telomerase in human fetal fibroblasts and somatic cells, extending their replicative lifespan beyond the Hayflick limit.
The compound is central to Khavinson's bioregulatory peptide theory of aging, which proposes that tissue-specific short peptides regulate gene expression and can counteract age-related decline.
researchContext: Published studies showed that Epitalon treatment elongated telomeres in human cell cultures and in aged individuals, though independent replication of these results is limited.
Animal studies (rodents, birds) showed increased lifespan and tumor suppression with epithalamin/Epitalon administration, though study designs have been criticized for small sample sizes.
The pineal gland connection links Epitalon to circadian rhythm regulation: it may restore age-related decline in melatonin production, contributing to improved sleep quality.
safetyNotes: Preclinical and early clinical data from Russian studies report no significant adverse effects. The tetrapeptide's simplicity contributes to expected low toxicity.
Theoretical concerns about telomerase activation in cancer-prone tissues exist, though Khavinson's research reported tumor-suppressive rather than tumor-promoting effects.

name: FOXO4-DRI
description: A cell-penetrating peptide that selectively induces apoptosis in senescent cells by disrupting the FOXO4-p53 interaction.
mechanismOfAction: FOXO4-DRI is a D-retro-inverso peptide that mimics a portion of the FOXO4 transcription factor. In senescent cells, FOXO4 binds to p53 and sequesters it in the nucleus, preventing p53-mediated apoptosis and allowing senescent cells to survive despite DNA damage. FOXO4-DRI competes with endogenous FOXO4 for p53 binding, releasing p53 to trigger apoptosis selectively in senescent cells. Non-senescent cells are unaffected because they do not depend on the FOXO4-p53 interaction for survival.

overview: FOXO4-DRI was developed by Peter de Keizer and colleagues at Erasmus University Medical Center in the Netherlands, published in Cell in 2017 as a targeted senolytic approach.

The D-retro-inverso design uses D-amino acids in reverse sequence to resist proteolytic degradation while maintaining binding specificity, a technique that dramatically improves peptide stability in vivo.
In aged mice, FOXO4-DRI treatment restored fitness, fur density, and renal function, demonstrating that selective senescent cell clearance can partially reverse aging phenotypes.
researchContext: The original Cell publication demonstrated selective killing of senescent cells in vitro and in vivo, with aged mice showing improved healthspan markers after treatment.
FOXO4-DRI is one of several senolytic strategies being explored, alongside small molecules like dasatinib + quercetin and navitoclax. The peptide approach offers greater target selectivity.
Clinical development has been pursued by de Keizer's company Numeric Biotech (formerly Cleara Biotech), with plans for human trials in senescence-driven diseases.
safetyNotes: Preclinical studies showed selectivity for senescent cells with minimal effects on non-senescent cells. However, systemic senolytic therapy requires careful evaluation of off-target effects.
The D-amino acid composition prevents immune recognition and enzymatic degradation but may also delay clearance. Long-term effects of senescent cell elimination are not fully understood.

name: VIP
description: A 28-amino acid neuropeptide with broad immunomodulatory, vasodilatory, and neuroprotective functions across multiple organ systems.
mechanismOfAction: VIP is a 28-amino acid neuropeptide of the secretin/glucagon superfamily that signals through VPAC1 and VPAC2 receptors (G-protein-coupled receptors activating adenylate cyclase). It produces vasodilation, bronchodilation, and smooth muscle relaxation. It modulates immune function by shifting T-cells toward Th2/regulatory phenotypes, inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12), and promoting immune tolerance. In the GI tract, it stimulates water and electrolyte secretion and relaxes smooth muscle.

overview: Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP) was first isolated from porcine small intestine by Sami Said and Viktor Mutt in 1970. It is widely distributed throughout the central and peripheral nervous system, GI tract, and immune system.

VIP functions as a neurotransmitter, neuromodulator, and immunoregulatory peptide. Its extreme functional diversity — spanning vasodilation, immunomodulation, neuroprotection, and GI regulation — makes it one of the most multi-functional endogenous peptides.
A synthetic form (aviptadil) was granted FDA Emergency Use Authorization during COVID-19 for acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), based on VIP's cytoprotective effects in lung tissue.
researchContext: VIP has been studied for chronic inflammatory airway disease (COPD, pulmonary hypertension), inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and neuroinflammatory conditions.
The ZYESAMI trial evaluated inhaled aviptadil for COVID-19-associated ARDS, based on VIP's ability to protect type 2 alveolar cells and modulate cytokine storm.
VIP's short half-life (1-2 minutes) has been a major limitation, driving research into sustained-release formulations and analogues with improved pharmacokinetics.
safetyNotes: VIP can cause transient hypotension, flushing, and tachycardia due to its vasodilatory effects. These effects are dose-dependent and typically self-limiting.
The very short half-life means that adverse effects resolve rapidly but also limits therapeutic utility without continuous infusion or sustained-release delivery.

name: 5-Amino-1MQ
description: A small-molecule NNMT inhibitor that promotes fat metabolism and cellular energy expenditure, studied for obesity and metabolic disease.
mechanismOfAction: 5-Amino-1MQ is a selective inhibitor of nicotinamide N-methyltransferase (NNMT), an enzyme that metabolizes NAD+ precursors and is upregulated in adipose tissue during obesity. By inhibiting NNMT, 5-Amino-1MQ increases intracellular NAD+ and SAM (S-adenosylmethionine) levels, promoting energy expenditure and reducing lipid accumulation in adipocytes. NNMT inhibition also affects the methionine cycle, influencing epigenetic regulation and cellular metabolism.

overview: 5-Amino-1MQ is a small-molecule NNMT inhibitor identified through research on metabolic regulators of adiposity. NNMT is highly expressed in white adipose tissue and serves as a metabolic rheostat linking the NAD+ salvage pathway to methylation metabolism.
Unlike peptide-based weight loss agents that suppress appetite centrally, 5-Amino-1MQ targets cellular metabolism directly, promoting fat cell energy expenditure and reducing fat storage capacity.
It is technically not a peptide but is included in the metabolic category due to its close intersection with the peptide research community and NAD+ biology.
researchContext: Preclinical studies in diet-induced obese mice showed that NNMT inhibition with 5-Amino-1MQ analogues reduced body weight and fat mass without affecting food intake, suggesting a metabolism-driven mechanism.
NNMT has emerged as a target of interest in both obesity and cancer research, as it regulates cellular energy balance and epigenetic marks.
No clinical trials have been conducted in humans. The compound remains a research tool for exploring NNMT biology.
safetyNotes: Preclinical toxicology data are limited. The compound's effects on NAD+ metabolism and methylation pathways require careful evaluation for potential off-target effects.
Human safety, dosing, and pharmacokinetic data are not available.

name: FTPP
description: A proapoptotic peptidomimetic that targets the vascular supply of adipose tissue, causing fat cell death through ischemia.
mechanismOfAction: FTPP (Adipotide) is a chimeric peptidomimetic consisting of two domains: a targeting sequence (CKGGRAKDC) that binds prohibitin on the endothelial cells of white adipose tissue vasculature, and a proapoptotic sequence D(KLAKLAK)2 that disrupts mitochondrial membranes upon internalization. By selectively destroying the blood supply to fat tissue, it causes adipocyte ischemia and fat pad reduction without systemic cytotoxicity.

overview: FTPP (Adipotide) was developed by Wadih Arap and Renata Pasqualini at MD Anderson Cancer Center, building on their vascular targeting work in cancer therapy.
In a study with obese rhesus macaques, Adipotide treatment resulted in approximately 11% body weight loss and 39% reduction in abdominal fat over 4 weeks.
The mechanism is unique among anti-obesity approaches: rather than reducing appetite or increasing metabolism, it physically destroys the vascular supply to adipose tissue.
researchContext: The primate study demonstrated proof-of-concept for vascular targeting in obesity, but also revealed significant renal toxicity that has limited further development.

The prohibitin target is expressed on vascular endothelium of white adipose tissue, providing tissue selectivity. However, prohibitin is also present on other vascular beds, raising off-target concerns.
No human clinical trials have been conducted due to the toxicity profile observed in primates.
safetyNotes: Significant renal toxicity was observed in the primate study, with elevated creatinine levels and evidence of tubular damage. This is the primary safety concern limiting development.
FTPP is a research compound only. Its mechanism of action — vascular destruction — carries inherent risks of tissue damage beyond the target site.

name: Copper Peptide Tripeptide-1
description: The INCI-standardized name for GHK-Cu in cosmetic formulations, used in professional skincare for collagen synthesis and wound healing.
mechanismOfAction: Copper peptide tripeptide-1 is the cosmetic industry standardized name (INCI nomenclature) for GHK-Cu (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine copper). The mechanism is identical to GHK-Cu: it delivers copper to tissues, stimulates collagen synthesis, promotes fibroblast and keratinocyte proliferation, and modulates gene expression related to tissue remodeling and anti-inflammatory processes.

overview: Copper peptide tripeptide-1 is the INCI (International Nomenclature of Cosmetic Ingredients) designation for GHK-Cu used in cosmetic product labeling. It is the same molecule as GHK-Cu discussed in the tissue-repair and cosmetic categories.
It appears on cosmetic ingredient lists and is regulated as a cosmetic ingredient rather than a pharmaceutical, allowing its inclusion in over-the-counter skincare products.
Professional-grade formulations typically contain 0.5-2% copper peptide tripeptide-1, with clinical evidence supporting anti-aging benefits at these concentrations.
researchContext: The cosmetic application of copper peptides has been validated in numerous clinical studies showing improvements in skin firmness, elasticity, wrinkle depth, and post-procedure healing.
Regulatory classification as a cosmetic ingredient means that efficacy claims are more limited than pharmaceutical claims, though the scientific evidence base is substantial.
Integration with other active cosmeceutical ingredients (retinoids, vitamin C, niacinamide) is a common formulation strategy, though stability with certain actives requires careful formulation.
safetyNotes: Copper peptide tripeptide-1 has extensive safety data from cosmetic use. It is well tolerated across skin types and does not cause photosensitization.
Avoid combining with strong acids (low-pH products) that may chelate the copper ion, reducing efficacy.

name: Pentadecapeptide
description: The generic term for the 15-amino acid peptide class, most commonly referring to BPC-157 in the tissue-repair context.
mechanismOfAction: Pentadecapeptide refers to any peptide composed of 15 amino acids. In the tissue-repair context, it almost exclusively refers to BPC-157, whose mechanism involves VEGF upregulation, eNOS modulation, FAK-paxillin pathway activation for cell migration, and modulation of multiple growth factor signaling cascades to promote healing across tissue types.

overview: Pentadecapeptide is the structural class name for peptides of 15 amino acid residues. In the peptide research and optimization community, it is nearly synonymous with BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157).
The term appears frequently in research literature and compounding pharmacy nomenclature as a generic descriptor for BPC-157.
This entry exists primarily to resolve the common naming confusion between the generic class term and the specific compound.
researchContext: The pentadecapeptide class name is most commonly encountered in Sikiric laboratory publications, where BPC-157 is formally described as a 'stable gastric pentadecapeptide.'
All published research on 'the pentadecapeptide' in the tissue-repair context refers to the BPC-157 sequence specifically.
No other pentadecapeptide has achieved comparable research attention in the tissue-repair category.
safetyNotes: Safety data are equivalent to BPC-157 (see BPC-157 entry). No unique safety considerations apply to the generic class name.
Users should verify that 'pentadecapeptide' products contain the specific BPC-157 sequence rather than an arbitrary 15-amino acid peptide.

name: Afamelanotide
description: The pharmaceutical form of Melanotan I, approved for erythropoietic protoporphyria as a subcutaneous implant for photoprotection.
mechanismOfAction: Afamelanotide is a 13-amino acid synthetic α-MSH analogue identical to Melanotan I. It selectively activates MC1R on melanocytes to upregulate eumelanin synthesis via cAMP/PKA/MITF signaling. The subcutaneous implant formulation provides sustained release over approximately 60 days, maintaining elevated melanin levels for photoprotection. It also provides DNA repair-promoting effects in melanocytes through MC1R-dependent pathways independent of pigmentation.

overview: Afamelanotide is the pharmaceutical-grade formulation of Melanotan I, developed by Clinuvel Pharmaceuticals as a biodegradable subcutaneous implant (Scenesse).
It is FDA-approved (2019) for prevention of phototoxicity in adults with erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP), a rare genetic condition causing extreme photosensitivity.
This entry provides the pharmaceutical/regulatory perspective on the same molecule covered under Melanotan I, reflecting its dual identity as a research compound and approved drug.
researchContext: Post-marketing studies continue to monitor long-term safety and efficacy of the implant formulation in EPP patients.
Clinuvel is exploring expanded indications including vitiligo, variegate porphyria, and arterial ischemic stroke, leveraging the neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory properties of MC1R activation.
The implant delivery system represents an important advance in peptide drug delivery, overcoming the short plasma half-life of the native peptide.
safetyNotes: Post-marketing surveillance confirms the safety profile from clinical trials: nausea, headache, and skin darkening are the most common effects.
Regular dermatological monitoring is recommended due to MC1R's role in melanocyte biology, though no melanoma signal has emerged in clinical studies.
